ai.0: Unit 4 Area of Study 1 is the story of three superb models — light as a wave, matter as particles, space and time as fixed — and the experiments that broke each of them. Read it in one sitting. Nothing here needs to be solved, only seen.
ai.1: Hold one question in mind for the whole of this page: what, exactly, is light? Newton said it was a stream of tiny particles — he called them corpuscles. His contemporary Huygens said it was a wave. The argument ran for more than a century, because both models explained the everyday evidence of reflection, refraction and shadows.
ai.2: The stakes are high: almost everything we know about the universe — the temperature of stars, the composition of galaxies, the structure of a virus — arrives as light. Get light wrong and you get everything wrong.
ai.3: This Area of Study — its official question is How has understanding about the physical world changed? — is built like a detective story. Each act opens with a confident model, introduces one stubborn experiment, and watches the model break. Figure 1.1 is the evidence board: stripes that particles cannot make, electrons ejected in a way waves cannot explain, and particles of matter drawing wave patterns. By the end, even space and time will have given way.
ai.4: Connects The Fields story ended with a promise: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field can regenerate each other, detach from every wire and charge, and travel through empty space — reread Where the field idea is heading. This page is that promise kept, and then broken, twice.
ai.5: In 1801 Thomas Young settled the old argument — apparently. He let light pass through two narrow slits onto a screen. If light were Newton's corpuscles, two bright stripes should appear, one behind each slit. Instead the screen showed many: a long ladder of evenly spaced bright and dark bands.
ai.6: Only a wave can do this. Where two ripples on a pond meet crest on crest, they reinforce; crest on trough, they cancel. A bright band marks a place where the waves from the two slits arrive in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ — and a dark band marks a half-wavelength of mismatch, (n + ½)λ, exactly as overlapping sound and water waves behave. The pattern is so lawful that one formula, Δx = λL/d on formula sheet, connects the fringe spacing to the wavelength — Young had measured the wavelength of light with little more than a ruler.
ai.7: But a wave of what? The answer came in the 1860s from territory you already own. James Clerk Maxwell showed that a changing electric field creates a changing magnetic field, which recreates the electric field, and that this self-sustaining pair must travel — at a speed his equations fixed at 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, exactly the measured speed of light. Light, then, can be modelled as a transverse electromagnetic wave, produced wherever charges accelerate, and every electromagnetic wave — radio to gamma — travels at the same speed c in a vacuum.
ai.8: You have already met the production line. Electrons forced around the Australian Synchrotron's storage ring are continuously accelerating — circular motion is acceleration — and accelerating charges radiate. That is why the machine glows. By 1900, with Young's stripes and Maxwell's fields in agreement, the case looked closed.
ai.9: Connects The wave model is built properly in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the interference evidence in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference. The fields doing the waving are the ones you mapped in Unit 3.
ai.10: The experiment that reopened the case looks harmless. Shine ultraviolet light on a clean metal surface and electrons jump out of it — the photoelectric effect.
ai.11: The wave model makes three confident predictions about it. Brighter light carries more energy, so the ejected electrons should leave faster. Any colour should work, given time for the metal to soak up energy. And in dim light there should be a delay while the energy accumulates.
ai.12: Experiment said no, three times. The maximum kinetic energy of the electrons depends only on the light's frequency — turning up the brightness ejects more electrons, never faster ones. Below a threshold frequency, no electrons leave at all, at any brightness. And emission is effectively instantaneous, even in the dimmest light.
ai.13: In 1905 Einstein proposed something the wave model could not survive: light delivers its energy in indivisible lumps. Each lump — each photon — carries an energy named by E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck's constant, an absurdly small 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. One photon gives all its energy to one electron, which pays the metal's exit fee — the work function φ — and keeps the change as kinetic energy: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet. Every awkward observation follows at a stroke: frequency sets the energy of each lump, brightness only counts the lumps, and a lump arrives whole or not at all.
ai.14: The measuring instrument is pure Unit 3. To find the electrons' energy, experimenters apply a reverse voltage and increase it until even the fastest electron no longer crosses — the stopping voltage — which is W = qV not on sheet run backwards, as a brake instead of an accelerator. And the electron-volt, the energy unit born when you accelerated charges through potential differences, becomes the working currency of everything that follows.
ai.15: Connects The photon and the full photoelectric analysis live in chapter 14 · The photon; the stopping-voltage idea and the electron-volt were banked in Electric fields · Work and energy.
ai.16: In 1924 a French physicist, Louis de Broglie, asked the symmetric question. If light — supposedly a wave — behaves as a stream of particles, might matter — supposedly particles — behave as a wave? He proposed that every moving object carries a wavelength, named by λ = h/p on formula sheet: the larger the momentum, the shorter the wave.
ai.17: Three years later the test was run: fire a beam of electrons at a thin crystal. The beam emerged drawing concentric rings on a screen — the same family of pattern that X-rays of equal wavelength make through the same crystal, as panel (c) of Figure 1.1 shows. The electron, the particle you accelerated with voltages and steered into circles with magnetic fields in Unit 3, diffracts.
ai.18: Why does a cricket ball not diffract through a doorway? Because h is so small. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ has a de Broglie wavelength of about 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m — unimaginably smaller than any gap it could ever pass through, and diffraction only becomes noticeable when the wavelength is comparable to the opening. Electrons are so light that their wavelengths match the spacing between atoms, which is why crystals make perfect diffraction gratings for them.
ai.19: The deepest version of the experiment is the one Simulation 1.1 lets you run. Send electrons — or photons — through Young's double slits one at a time. Each arrives at the screen as a single localised dot: a particle. But as thousands of dots accumulate, the interference fringes assemble themselves out of the statistics: a wave — no single particle makes a pattern, yet every particle obeys one.
ai.20: Connects Matter waves get their own chapter at chapter 15 · Matter waves. The electron beam that stars in it is the one you built in Motors & accelerators — same electrons, new identity.
ai.21: Two awkward facts about atoms were waiting for these ideas. First, atoms should not exist: a classical electron circling a nucleus is accelerating, and accelerating charges radiate — Maxwell's own rule, the one that lights the synchrotron — so the electron should broadcast its energy away and spiral into the nucleus in a fraction of a second.
ai.22: Second, glowing gases do not emit a rainbow. Each element emits only certain sharp colours — a line spectrum, a barcode unique to that element. Sodium street lamps glow with their twin yellow lines; metal vapour lamps and neon signs each show their own barcode; sunlight carries thousands of dark lines where cooler gas has subtracted exactly those colours.
ai.23: The explanation is quantisation. An electron bound in an atom can hold only certain energies — rungs on a ladder, with nothing in between. Drop from a higher rung to a lower one and the atom emits a single photon whose energy E = hf exactly matches the gap; absorb a photon of exactly a gap's energy and the electron climbs. Bright emission lines and dark absorption lines are the same ladder read in opposite directions, and reading a spectrum becomes reading the atom's energy levels.
ai.24: But why rungs at all? De Broglie's wave answers this too. Wrap the electron's wave around the atom and it must close on itself smoothly — a standing wave, like a guitar string that can play only its harmonics. Only certain wavelengths fit the loop, so only certain energies are allowed.
ai.25: The picture is a simplified model, refined by later physics, but it explains the one thing the classical atom never could: why an atom has a lowest rung — a ground state — below which the electron cannot fall, and therefore why atoms are stable. Quantisation is not a quirk of light; it is the architecture of matter.
ai.26: Connects Spectra and energy levels are read properly in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; the standing waves that make the rungs are rehearsed with light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
ai.27: One loose thread remained from the wave's first victory. Every wave known to physics waves in something — so what carries light across empty space? Physicists named the proposed medium the aether, and in 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument exquisite enough to find it: two light beams raced along perpendicular arms, recombined to interfere, the whole apparatus turned and re-tried through the seasons as Earth swung around the Sun. If Earth moves through an aether, the beam times should differ — they never did, the most famous null result in physics.
ai.28: In 1905 — the same year as the photon — Einstein took the result at face value and rebuilt mechanics from two postulates: the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, regardless of the motion of the source or the observer. The second postulate sounds innocent. It is not. If everyone measures the same c, then time and distance themselves must differ between observers.
ai.29: The consequences carry names you will use all year. Moving clocks run slow — time dilation, named by t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths shrink along the direction of travel — length contraction, named by L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
ai.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, is barely above 1 at everyday speeds — which is why nobody noticed for three centuries — and grows without limit as speed approaches c. The fine print "v << c" attached to Unit 3's circular-path formula was this warning, planted a unit early.
ai.31: The evidence falls out of the sky. Muons — heavy, short-lived cousins of the electron, created kilometres overhead by cosmic rays — live about 2.2 μs on average. Even at 0.995c that allows roughly 660 m of travel, yet they arrive at the ground in abundance. In the ground frame of reference their internal clocks run about ten times slow, so they live long enough to make the trip; in the muon's frame of reference the atmosphere is contracted about tenfold, so the trip is short enough to survive — two descriptions, one agreed outcome.
ai.32: And the satellites that locate your phone carry clocks that tick slow by a few microseconds each day because of their orbital speed — left uncorrected, GPS fixes would drift by kilometres every day. Your phone applies special relativity every time it finds you.
ai.33: Connects Postulates, proper time and the muon story are built in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. The "v << c" fine print was planted on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
ai.34: Einstein's relativity papers of 1905 ended with a short afterthought that outgrew everything else: mass is a concentrated form of energy. The rest energy locked in any object is named by E0 = mc² on formula sheet, and because the exchange rate is c², a teaspoon of water holds enough rest energy to supply twenty thousand homes with electricity for a year. Set the object moving and its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, with the kinetic share named by Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet — at everyday speeds this collapses back to the familiar formula, one more approximation with its fine print now visible.
ai.35: The Sun runs on this exchange. In its core, hydrogen fuses into helium, and the products weigh slightly less than the ingredients; the missing mass leaves as light. The Sun converts about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiated energy every second — sunshine is the Sun's mass, spent at the universal rate.
ai.36: The exchange runs in both directions. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, both vanish and two gamma-ray photons fly apart in opposite directions — opposite, because momentum must still balance. Hospital PET scanners image the body by catching exactly those photon pairs.
ai.37: And in particle accelerators the trade reverses: the kinetic energy of a collision condenses into new particles that did not exist before the impact. The synchrotron you studied as a circular-motion exercise is, in the fine print, a machine whose every magnet is engineered around γ.
ai.38: Connects The full energy ledger is settled in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that spend it were built in Motors & accelerators.
ai.39: You have now read the whole Area of Study once: a wave that turned out to arrive in lumps, particles that turned out to interfere, atoms saved by standing waves, and a speed limit that bends time and length and reveals mass as concentrated energy. Everything from here is the same story at higher resolution.
ai.40: Pass 2 carries it in six chapters, numbered 12 to 17, each meant to be read the night before class meets the topic: light as a wave; diffraction and interference; the photon; matter waves; atoms and spectra; relativity and mass–energy. There you will find every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and quick checks to confirm each idea landed.
ai.41: Pass 3 is for exam season. Four pages walk the twenty-eight key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — light as a wave; light and matter as particles; light–matter similarities; relativity and mass–energy — with the subtleties, the classic traps, the assessors' advice and exam-style questions with full solutions. The depth row below carries you down whenever you are ready, and this page stays one click away for whenever the detail starts to obscure the story.

socratic.0:
Socrates. Let us begin together, you and I, with three convictions long held to be settled: that light is a wave, that matter is made of particles, and that space and time are fixed for all. Tell me — would it trouble you to learn that experiment overturned every one of them?
Student. It would surprise me, certainly. Are we to prove this here?
Socrates. Not to prove — we have nothing to solve. We need only walk over the whole terrain once, as one walks a field before working it, and see how each conviction fell.
socratic.1:
Socrates. So tell me first: what is light?
Student. I should have to think. Some have said it is a stream of tiny particles.
Socrates. Just so — Newton said exactly that, and called them corpuscles. But his contemporary Huygens insisted it was a wave. Now, which of them was right?
Student. Surely one could simply test it and see.
Socrates. One would think so. Yet the dispute ran more than a century. Why, do you suppose, was it so hard to settle?
Student. Perhaps both pictures explained the same things?
Socrates. Precisely. Both accounted for reflection, refraction, and the casting of shadows. The everyday evidence could not choose between them.
socratic.2:
Socrates. And does it much matter which picture we hold? It is, after all, only light.
Student. I want to say it matters little — but I suspect you are leading me somewhere.
Socrates. Then consider: how do you know the temperature of a star? The substances in a distant galaxy? The very shape of a virus?
Student. All of those reach us as light, do they not.
Socrates. Almost all that we know of the universe arrives so. And if our account of light is wrong —?
Student. Then our account of everything resting upon it is wrong too. I see it is not a small matter at all.
socratic.3:
Socrates. The official question we are set is this — How has understanding about the physical world changed? — and we shall answer it in stages. Can you guess the shape each stage will take?
Student. Begin with the accepted model, I would think.
Socrates. Yes — open with a settled model, set against it one stubborn experimental result, and follow the model until it breaks. Figure 1.1 lays out the evidence we shall meet: stripes no particle could make, electrons flung off in a way no wave can explain, and particles of matter that draw wave patterns. And by the end —?
Student. You hinted already. Space and time themselves are revised.
Socrates. They are.
socratic.4:
Connects Socrates. Cast your mind back to where the Fields material left us. What was its parting result?
Student. That a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field can regenerate each other, break free of every wire and charge, and travel through empty space — it is set out in Where the field idea is heading.
Socrates. That very travelling thing is what this page takes up. And then watches overturned — twice over.
socratic.5:
Socrates. Now to the experiment many thought decisive. In 1801 Thomas Young sent light through two narrow slits onto a screen. If Newton were right, and light were corpuscles, what should appear on that screen?
Student. Two bright stripes, I should think — one behind each slit, where the particles land.
Socrates. A reasonable expectation. But that is not what Young saw. He found not two stripes but many — a long row of evenly spaced bright and dark bands.
Student. Many bands? From only two slits? Particles could never do that.
socratic.6:
Socrates. They could not. What, then, can?
Student. A wave, surely — like ripples meeting on a pond.
Socrates. Tell me what ripples do when they meet.
Student. Crest on crest, they reinforce; crest on trough, they cancel.
Socrates. So a bright band marks —?
Student. Where the two waves arrive in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ.
Socrates. And a dark band?
Student. A half-wavelength of mismatch — (n + ½)λ — just as overlapping sound and water waves behave.
Socrates. And the pattern is so regular that a single formula, Δx = λL/d on formula sheet, ties the fringe spacing to the wavelength. So with little more than a ruler, what had Young in fact measured?
Student. The wavelength of light itself.
socratic.7:
Socrates. If light is a wave, one ought to ask: what is waving?
Student. For ripples it is water. For light I have no answer.
Socrates. Nor had anyone, until the 1860s, when Maxwell built the answer from what was already known. He showed that a changing electric field makes a changing magnetic field, which remakes the electric field — and that this self-sustaining pair must travel. At what speed, do you suppose, did his equations fix it?
Student. I cannot guess the number.
Socrates. 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ — the measured speed of light, exactly. What would you conclude?
Student. That light is that very pair — a transverse electromagnetic wave.
Socrates. Produced wherever charges accelerate. And every such wave, from radio to gamma, travels at the one speed c in a vacuum.
socratic.8:
Socrates. You have met this production already, though you may not recall where. Think of the Australian Synchrotron — electrons forced round its storage ring. Are they accelerating?
Student. Circular motion is acceleration, yes — even at constant speed the direction turns.
Socrates. And accelerating charges?
Student. Radiate. So that is why the machine pours out light.
Socrates. Just so. By 1900, with Young's pattern and Maxwell's fields in agreement, would you have called the matter closed?
Student. I would have. Light is a wave, and there's an end of it.
socratic.9:
Connects Socrates. Where might you pursue this wave picture in its full depth?
Student. The wave model in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the interference evidence in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference.
Socrates. And the fields beneath it all are those you mapped in Unit 3.
socratic.10:
Socrates. But you spoke too soon in closing the matter. A second experiment reopened it — and it looks utterly plain. Shine ultraviolet light on a clean metal surface. What happens?
Student. Something is given off?
Socrates. Electrons are ejected from the metal. This is the photoelectric effect. Plain enough — yet within it the wave model meets its ruin.
socratic.11:
Socrates. Let us hold to the wave model and reason out what it predicts. First: brighter light — what does it carry?
Student. More energy. So the electrons should come off faster.
Socrates. Second: must the light be of any particular colour?
Student. I see no reason. Any colour should serve, if the metal is given time to soak up enough energy.
Socrates. And third, in very dim light?
Student. There should be a delay — a pause while the energy gathers.
socratic.12:
Socrates. Three clean predictions. Now hear what experiment reported. The greatest kinetic energy of the electrons depends on one thing only — the light's frequency. Brightness changes nothing of their speed; it only ejects more of them. What of your first prediction?
Student. Overturned. Brighter light should have meant faster, and it did not.
Socrates. Below a certain threshold frequency, no electron emerges at all — however blinding the brightness. Your second?
Student. Also wrong. Some colours simply will not do, at any intensity.
Socrates. And emission is effectively instant, even in the faintest light. Your third?
Student. Gone as well. There is no delay. The wave model has failed on all three counts.
socratic.13:
Socrates. So in 1905 Einstein offered an account no wave could supply: light delivers its energy in indivisible amounts. Each amount — each photon — carries an energy E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck's constant, 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. Now reason with me. One photon gives all its energy to one electron, which must first pay the metal's exit price — the work function φ — and keeps what remains. So the electron's energy is —?
Student. The photon's energy less the toll: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet.
Socrates. Now see if your three puzzles dissolve. Why does frequency alone set the energy?
Student. Because E = hf fixes each amount. Brightness only sends more amounts — more electrons, not faster ones. And an amount arrives whole or not at all, so a too-feeble photon does nothing, and there is no waiting. Every observation follows.
socratic.14:
Socrates. One question of method. How would you measure how fast the fastest electron flies?
Student. I am not sure one can catch it directly.
Socrates. Then turn a tool from Unit 3 against it. Apply a reverse voltage and raise it until even the swiftest electron can no longer cross. What have you found at that point?
Student. The stopping voltage — it is W = qV not on sheet, but run backwards, a brake rather than an accelerator.
Socrates. And the energy unit born when charges were accelerated through potential differences — the electron-volt — becomes from here onward the working currency of all that follows.
socratic.15:
Connects Socrates. Where is the photon's full reckoning laid out?
Student. The photon and the whole photoelectric analysis in chapter 14 · The photon; and the stopping voltage and the electron-volt were first set down in Electric fields · Work and energy.
Socrates. Good. Keep both within reach.
socratic.16:
Socrates. Now turn the question on its head. We have seen light, called a wave, behave as a stream of particles. In 1924 de Broglie asked the mirror question. Can you frame it yourself?
Student. If a wave can act as particles, then perhaps matter — which we call particles — can act as a wave.
Socrates. Exactly his thought. He proposed that every moving thing carries a wavelength, λ = h/p on formula sheet. And as the momentum grows?
Student. The wave shortens. So the heavier and faster a thing, the smaller its wave.
socratic.17:
Socrates. Three years on, the wager was tested: a beam of electrons fired at a thin crystal. What do you imagine emerged on the screen?
Student. If de Broglie is right — a wave pattern?
Socrates. Concentric rings — the very same family of pattern that X-rays of equal wavelength make through that same crystal, as panel (c) of Figure 1.1 shows. So tell me what the electron has done.
Student. The electron — the same particle accelerated by voltages and steered into circles by magnetic fields in Unit 3 — has diffracted. Matter waves.
socratic.18:
Socrates. Then why does a cricket ball not diffract as it passes through a doorway?
Student. I suppose its wave is too small to notice — because h is so very small.
Socrates. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ has a de Broglie wavelength near 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m. Compare that to any doorway.
Student. Vanishingly smaller. And diffraction shows only when the wavelength is near the size of the opening.
Socrates. So why do electrons oblige where cricket balls will not?
Student. They are so light that their wavelengths match the spacing between atoms — which is why a crystal works as a diffraction grating for them.
socratic.19:
Socrates. Now the deepest form of the experiment, the one Simulation 1.1 shows. Send electrons — or photons — through Young's two slits, but one at a time. How does each one arrive?
Student. As a single dot, I would expect — a particle striking one spot.
Socrates. A single localised dot, yes. Now let thousands gather. What forms?
Student. Surely just a smudge behind the slits?
Socrates. The interference fringes form — out of the statistics alone. No single particle makes a pattern, yet every particle obeys one. Does that not sit strangely with you?
Student. It does. Each is a particle, and all together are a wave. I am not sure I can picture how both are true.
socratic.20:
Connects Socrates. Where is this strangeness given its full treatment?
Student. Matter waves in chapter 15 · Matter waves — and the electron beam there is the one built in Motors & accelerators.
Socrates. The same electrons, cast in a new role.
socratic.21:
Socrates. Let us bring these new ideas to bear on the atom, for two old wounds were waiting there. The first concerns stability. On the classical account, picture an electron circling a nucleus. Is it accelerating?
Student. Yes — it is forever turning, so yes.
Socrates. And an accelerating charge, by Maxwell's own rule — the one that lights the synchrotron —?
Student. Radiates. So the electron should bleed away its energy and spiral into the nucleus.
Socrates. In how long, do you think?
Student. If the rule holds, a mere fraction of a second. Yet atoms plainly endure. That should not be possible.
socratic.22:
Socrates. The second wound concerns the light a glowing gas gives off. Would you expect a smooth spread of every colour?
Student. I would have, like a rainbow.
Socrates. Yet each element emits only certain sharp colours — a line spectrum, unique to it like a signature. Sodium street lamps show their two yellow lines; metal-vapour lamps and neon signs each their own; and sunlight is crossed by thousands of dark lines, where cooler gas has stolen exactly those colours.
Student. So the colours are picked out, not poured out. Why only those, and no others?
socratic.23:
Socrates. The answer is quantisation. Suppose an electron bound in an atom may hold only certain energies — discrete levels, with nothing between. Now, what must happen when it drops from a higher level to a lower?
Student. It must shed the difference. As a photon, I expect.
Socrates. A single photon whose energy E = hf matches the gap exactly. And to climb?
Student. It must absorb a photon of precisely that gap's energy.
Socrates. So the bright emission lines and the dark absorption lines —?
Student. Are the same set of levels read in opposite directions. The spectrum lays bare the atom's energy levels.
socratic.24:
Socrates. And here de Broglie's wave returns to earn its keep. Why should the levels be discrete at all? Picture the electron's wave wrapped round the atom. What must it do to survive?
Student. Close on itself smoothly, I suppose — meet its own tail.
Socrates. A standing wave, like a string that sounds only its harmonics. And so?
Student. Only certain wavelengths fit the loop — and therefore only certain energies are allowed. That is why the levels are discrete.
socratic.25:
Socrates. This is a simplified picture, mind — later physics refines it. But it earns the one thing the classical atom could never explain. Can you name it?
Student. Why an atom has a lowest level — a ground state — beneath which the electron cannot fall.
Socrates. And from that, what follows?
Student. That atoms are stable. The electron simply has no lower step to spiral down to.
Socrates. So you see quantisation is not light's affair alone.
Student. No — it runs through matter itself.
socratic.26:
Connects Socrates. And where shall you find the spectra worked out fully?
Student. Spectra and energy levels in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; and the standing waves that make the levels are introduced with light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
socratic.27:
Socrates. One loose thread still trails from the wave model's first triumph. Every wave you know waves in something. So light, crossing empty space, waves in —?
Student. I cannot say. There is nothing out there to wave.
Socrates. The physicists supposed there must be, and named it the aether. In 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument fine enough to catch it: two light beams ran down perpendicular arms, recombined to interfere, and the whole was turned and tested again through the seasons as Earth swept round the Sun. If Earth moves through an aether, what should they have found?
Student. The beam times should differ.
Socrates. They never did — not once. The most famous null result in all of physics.
socratic.28:
Socrates. In 1905 — the same year as the photon — Einstein did not explain the null result away. He took it as true and rebuilt mechanics on two postulates. First, the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame. Second, the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, whatever the motion of source or observer. Does that second one trouble you?
Student. It sounds harmless enough.
Socrates. Then chase it. If every observer, however he moves, measures the very same c — what must give way?
Student. If the speed cannot change between them... then the time and the distance must. Time and length themselves differ from observer to observer.
socratic.29:
Socrates. And these consequences carry names you will use all year. A clock in motion — what does it do?
Student. Runs slow. Time dilation — t = γt0 on formula sheet.
Socrates. And a length in motion?
Student. Shrinks along the direction it travels. Length contraction — L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
socratic.30:
Socrates. That factor γ — the Lorentz factor — how large is it at the speeds of ordinary life?
Student. Only a hair above 1, I would guess, since no one noticed these effects for centuries.
Socrates. Just barely above 1. And as speed climbs toward c?
Student. It grows without limit.
Socrates. Recall the qualification "v << c" pinned to Unit 3's circular-path formula. What was it quietly warning you of?
Student. Of exactly this — a unit before I could see why.
socratic.31:
Socrates. You may doubt all this. So where can we see it in nature? Consider the muon — a heavy, short-lived cousin of the electron, made kilometres overhead by cosmic rays. It lives, on average, about 2.2 μs. Even at 0.995c, how far should it get?
Student. Roughly 660 m, by that lifetime. So it should never reach the ground.
Socrates. And yet muons arrive at the surface in great numbers. How, in the ground's frame?
Student. Their internal clocks run about ten times slow — so they live long enough to finish the trip.
Socrates. And in the muon's own frame, where its clock is normal?
Student. Then the atmosphere is contracted about tenfold — so the journey is short enough to finish. Two descriptions, one outcome.
socratic.32:
Socrates. You think this remote from your life. Yet how does your phone know where you stand?
Student. By the satellites overhead.
Socrates. Whose clocks, owing to their orbital speed, run slow by a few microseconds each day. Leave that uncorrected, and what becomes of your position?
Student. It would drift by kilometres a day. So my phone is quietly applying special relativity every time it finds me.
Socrates. Every single time.
socratic.33:
Connects Socrates. And where is all this set out in full?
Student. The postulates, proper time, and the muon evidence in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy — and that "v << c" qualification was first met on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
socratic.34:
Socrates. Einstein's 1905 papers ended on a brief result that outweighed all the rest: mass is a concentrated form of energy. The rest energy in any object is E0 = mc² on formula sheet. Now, the conversion factor is c² — an enormous number. What does that imply for a mere teaspoon of water?
Student. If c² multiplies it... a great deal of energy.
Socrates. Enough to supply twenty thousand homes with electricity for a year. And set the object moving — its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, with the moving share Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet. And at everyday speeds?
Student. It collapses back to the familiar kinetic-energy formula — another approximation whose small print I now understand.
socratic.35:
Socrates. The Sun lives by this very conversion. In its core hydrogen fuses into helium — and the products weigh slightly less than the reactants. Where has the missing mass gone?
Student. Emitted as light, I should think — turned into energy.
Socrates. About 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of the Sun's own substance, converted to radiated energy each second. So when sunlight warms your face —?
Student. I am feeling the Sun's mass itself, converted at the universal rate. That is a strange thought.
socratic.36:
Socrates. And the conversion runs both ways. Let an electron meet its antiparticle, the positron. What becomes of them?
Student. Both are annihilated — turned to energy.
Socrates. Into two gamma-ray photons, flying apart in opposite directions. Why opposite, do you suppose?

Socrates. Just so. And hospital PET scanners image the body by catching exactly these photon pairs.

feynman.0: Unit 4 Area of Study 1 is the story of three ideas everyone was sure about — that light is a wave, that matter is made of particles, and that space and time just sit there, fixed — and the experiments that knocked every one of them over. Read it straight through, like a story. There’s nothing to solve here.
feynman.1: The question that runs underneath this whole page is a simple one: what is light? Newton said it was a stream of tiny particles — he called them corpuscles. His contemporary Huygens said no, it’s a wave. And the argument dragged on for more than a hundred years, because here’s the trouble: both pictures explained the everyday stuff just fine — reflection, refraction, shadows. Neither one could be ruled out.
feynman.2: Why does this matter so much? Because almost everything we know about the universe — how hot a star is, what a galaxy is made of, the shape of a virus — arrives at us as light. So if you get light wrong, you get everything that rides on it wrong too.
feynman.3: This Area of Study — its official question is How has understanding about the physical world changed? — goes in stages. Each stage starts with a model everybody trusted, drops in one stubborn experimental result that won’t go away, and then follows that model right to the point where it breaks. Figure 1.1 lays out the evidence: stripes that particles simply can’t make, electrons kicked out in a way no wave can explain, and bits of matter drawing wave patterns. And by the end, even space and time themselves get rewritten.
feynman.4: Connects The Fields material left us with a beautiful result: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field can keep making each other, peel away from every wire and charge, and go sailing off through empty space — see Where the field idea is heading. This page takes that result and runs with it — and then watches it get overturned, twice.
feynman.5: In 1801 Thomas Young looked like he’d settled the whole argument once and for all. He sent light through two narrow slits onto a screen. Now if light were really Newton’s corpuscles, you’d expect two bright stripes, one sitting behind each slit. But that’s not what showed up. The screen was covered in stripes — a long row of evenly spaced bright and dark bands.
feynman.6: Only a wave does that. Think of two ripples spreading on a pond: where crest meets crest they pile up, and where crest meets trough they wipe each other out. A bright band sits where the waves from the two slits arrive perfectly in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ — and a dark band sits where they’re out by half a wavelength, (n + ½)λ, exactly the way sound waves and water waves behave when they overlap. And the pattern is so neat and regular that one little formula, Δx = λL/d on formula sheet, ties the spacing of the fringes to the wavelength. So Young actually measured the wavelength of light with not much more than a ruler.
feynman.7: But a wave of what? That got answered in the 1860s, and the answer was already lying around. James Clerk Maxwell showed that a changing electric field makes a changing magnetic field, which makes the electric field again, and that this self-feeding pair simply has to travel — at a speed his equations pinned down at 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, which is exactly the measured speed of light. So light can be pictured as a transverse electromagnetic wave, made wherever charges accelerate, and every electromagnetic wave — from radio all the way up to gamma — goes at that same speed c in a vacuum.
feynman.8: You’ve already met a real machine that does this. Electrons whipped around the Australian Synchrotron’s storage ring are accelerating the whole time — going in a circle is accelerating — and accelerating charges radiate. That’s where the machine’s light comes from. So by 1900, with Young’s pattern and Maxwell’s fields both pointing the same way, the case looked closed.
feynman.9: Connects The wave model is worked out in full in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the interference evidence in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference. The fields in play are the ones mapped out in Unit 3.
feynman.10: The experiment that cracked the case back open looks like nothing special. Shine ultraviolet light on a clean metal surface and electrons come flying off it — that’s the photoelectric effect.
feynman.11: The wave model makes three confident predictions about it. Brighter light carries more energy, so the electrons that come off ought to come off faster. Any colour ought to work, as long as you give the metal time to soak up enough energy. And in dim light there ought to be a delay while the energy slowly builds up.
feynman.12: And the experiment said no to every single one. The maximum kinetic energy of the electrons depends only on the light’s frequency; turning up the brightness gives you more electrons, not faster ones. Below a threshold frequency, you get no electrons at all, no matter how bright you make the light. And the electrons come off basically the instant the light hits, even in the dimmest light you can manage.
feynman.13: In 1905 Einstein floated an idea the wave model just couldn’t swallow: light hands over its energy in indivisible lumps. Each lump — each photon — carries an energy E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck’s constant, 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. One photon dumps all its energy into one electron, which spends the metal’s exit fee — the work function φ — and keeps whatever’s left over as kinetic energy: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet. And look how everything falls out of this: frequency sets how big each lump is, brightness only sets how many lumps there are, and a lump goes over whole or not at all.
feynman.14: How do you measure the electrons’ energy? The trick comes straight from Unit 3. You put a voltage the wrong way round and crank it up until even the fastest electron can’t make it across — that’s the stopping voltage — which is just W = qV not on sheet run in reverse, working as a brake instead of an accelerator. And the electron-volt — the energy unit you defined when you pushed charges through potential differences — becomes the everyday currency for everything that follows.
feynman.15: Connects The photon and the full photoelectric story are handled in chapter 14 · The photon; the stopping-voltage idea and the electron-volt were set up in Electric fields · Work and energy.
feynman.16: In 1924 a French physicist, Louis de Broglie, asked the question the other way around. If light — which we thought was a wave — goes around acting like a stream of particles, then maybe matter — which we thought was particles — goes around acting like a wave. He said every moving thing carries a wavelength, given by λ = h/p on formula sheet: the more momentum, the shorter the wave.
feynman.17: Three years later somebody tried it: fire a beam of electrons at a thin crystal. Out the other side came concentric rings on a screen — the very same kind of pattern X-rays of the same wavelength make going through the same crystal, as panel (c) of Figure 1.1 shows. The electron — the very particle you accelerated with voltages and bent into circles with magnetic fields back in Unit 3 — diffracts.
feynman.18: So why doesn’t a cricket ball diffract through a doorway? Because h is so tiny. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ has a de Broglie wavelength of about 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m — way, way smaller than any gap it could ever fit through, and you only notice diffraction when the wavelength is roughly the size of the opening. Electrons, though, are light enough that their wavelengths come out about the size of the spacing between atoms — which is exactly why crystals work as diffraction gratings for them.
feynman.19: The deepest version of the whole experiment is the one Simulation 1.1 shows you. Send electrons — or photons — through Young’s double slits one at a time. Each one lands on the screen as a single little dot: a particle, no question. But let thousands of dots pile up, and the interference fringes build themselves right out of the statistics: a wave. No single particle makes a pattern, and yet every single particle somehow obeys one.
feynman.20: Connects Matter waves are handled in chapter 15 · Matter waves. The electron beam you’ll see there is the one built in Motors & accelerators — the same electrons, playing a new part.
feynman.21: Two nagging facts about atoms came before all these ideas. First, on the old classical picture atoms shouldn’t even hold together: a classical electron circling a nucleus is accelerating, and accelerating charges radiate — that’s Maxwell’s own rule, the very one that explains the synchrotron’s light — so the electron ought to radiate its energy away and spiral into the nucleus in a tiny fraction of a second.
feynman.22: Second, glowing gases don’t pour out a smooth rainbow. Each element gives off only certain sharp colours — a line spectrum, a fingerprint of that element. Sodium street lamps glow with their two yellow lines; metal vapour lamps and neon signs each flash their own set of lines; and sunlight is laced with thousands of dark lines where cooler gas has stolen exactly those colours back.
feynman.23: The explanation is quantisation. An electron stuck in an atom is only allowed to have certain energies — separate levels, with nothing in between. Drop from a higher level to a lower one and the atom spits out a single photon whose energy E = hf exactly matches the gap; soak up a photon carrying exactly a gap’s worth of energy and the electron jumps up a level. Bright emission lines and dark absorption lines are the same set of levels run in opposite directions — so a spectrum is really a readout of the atom’s energy levels.
feynman.24: And de Broglie’s wave tells you why the levels are separate in the first place. Wrap the electron’s wave around the atom and it has to join up smoothly with itself — a standing wave, just like a string that’ll only sing its harmonics. Only certain wavelengths fit neatly around the loop, so only certain energies are allowed.
feynman.25: It’s a simplified picture, cleaned up by later physics, but it nails the one thing the classical atom never could: why an atom has a lowest level — a ground state — that the electron can’t drop below, and so why atoms hold together at all. And quantisation isn’t just a light thing; it’s built right into matter.
feynman.26: Connects Spectra and energy levels are handled in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; the standing waves that make the levels first show up alongside light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
feynman.27: There was one loose thread left over from the wave model’s first big win. Every wave physics knew about waves in something — so it was natural to ask what carries light across empty space. Physicists named the supposed medium the aether, and in 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument fine enough to catch it: two light beams ran down arms at right angles, came back together to interfere, and they spun the whole apparatus around and retested it through the seasons as Earth swung around the Sun. If Earth is moving through an aether, the two beams should take different times; they never did — the most famous nothing in all of physics.
feynman.28: In 1905 — the same year as the photon — Einstein took that result at face value and rebuilt all of mechanics on just two postulates: the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, no matter how the source or the observer is moving. The second one sounds harmless, but it isn’t. If everybody measures the very same c, then time and distance themselves have to come out different for different observers.
feynman.29: The fallout has names you’ll use all year. Moving clocks run slow — time dilation, t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths shrink along the direction they’re travelling — length contraction, L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
feynman.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, sits just a hair above 1 at everyday speeds — which is exactly why nobody spotted any of this for three centuries — and then shoots off without limit as you push toward c. That little “v << c” tucked onto Unit 3’s circular-path formula was hinting at this condition, a whole unit ahead of time.
feynman.31: Where do we actually see it? In cosmic rays. Muons — heavy, short-lived cousins of the electron, made kilometres up by cosmic rays — live about 2.2 μs on average. Even at 0.995c that’s only good for roughly 660 m of travel, and yet muons pile up at the ground in huge numbers. In the ground frame of reference their internal clocks tick about ten times slow, so they last long enough to finish the trip; in the muon’s frame of reference the atmosphere is squashed about tenfold, so the trip is short enough to finish — two stories, one and the same answer.
feynman.32: The satellites that pin down your phone carry clocks that run slow by a few microseconds a day because of their orbital speed; leave that uncorrected and GPS would have you drifting off by kilometres a day. So your phone is doing special relativity every single time it figures out where you are.
feynman.33: Connects The postulates, proper time and the muon evidence are worked out in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. That “v << c” qualification first turned up on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
feynman.34: Einstein’s 1905 relativity papers ended with one short result that turned out to outshine everything else in them: mass is just a very concentrated form of energy. The rest energy packed into any object is E0 = mc² on formula sheet, and because the conversion factor is that whopping c², a teaspoon of water holds enough rest energy to power twenty thousand homes for a year. Get the object moving and its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, with the kinetic part being Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet — and at everyday speeds this collapses back to the familiar formula, one more approximation whose fine print you can now read.
feynman.35: The Sun runs on exactly this conversion. Down in its core, hydrogen fuses into helium, and the leftovers weigh a touch less than what went in; that missing mass goes out as light. The Sun turns about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiated energy every second — sunlight is literally the Sun’s own mass, converted at the universal rate.
feynman.36: And it works both ways. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, both vanish and two gamma-ray photons go shooting off in opposite directions — opposite, because momentum still has to balance out. Hospital PET scanners take pictures of your insides by catching exactly these photon pairs.
feynman.37: In particle accelerators the conversion runs backwards: the kinetic energy of a collision gets turned into brand-new particles that didn’t exist before the smash. The synchrotron you met earlier as a tidy circular-motion exercise is, when you look closer, a machine with every magnet engineered around γ.
feynman.38: Connects The full energy story is worked out in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that supply that energy were handled in Motors & accelerators.
feynman.39: So you’ve now read the whole Area of Study once through: a wave that turned out to come in discrete lumps, particles that turned out to interfere, atoms held together by standing waves, and a speed limit that bends time and length and reveals mass to be concentrated energy. What comes next is the same material, dug into more deeply.
feynman.40: Pass 2 builds it out across six chapters, numbered 6 to 11, each one meant to be read the night before class hits that topic: light as a wave; diffraction and interference; the photon; matter waves; atoms and spectra; relativity and mass–energy. Each one carries every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and quick checks so you can be sure each idea has actually landed.
feynman.41: Pass 3 is for getting ready for the exam. Four pages cover the twenty-eight key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — light as a wave; light and matter as particles; light–matter similarities; relativity and mass–energy — with the subtleties, the common slip-ups, the assessors’ advice, and exam-style questions worked all the way through. The depth row below takes you down when you need it, and this page is always one click away whenever the detail starts hiding the big picture.
feynman.42: Connects Pass 2 kicks off at Pass 2 · chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and you can bookmark Pass 3 · Light as a wave for when exam prep comes around.

confucius-en.0: The Master said. This study holds up three beliefs that men once thought settled — that light is a wave, that matter is made of particles, and that space and time stand fixed — and the experiments by which each was, in turn, undone. Read it as one continuous account, from beginning to end. Here nothing is to be solved; only to be seen.
confucius-en.1: The question that orders this whole page is a single one: what is the nature of light? Newton held it to be a stream of tiny grains, which he named corpuscles. His contemporary Huygens held it to be a wave. For more than a hundred years the dispute would not rest, for each model could account well enough for what the eye sees every day — reflection, refraction, and the casting of shadows. When the evidence will serve two masters, the wise do not yet choose.
confucius-en.2: Weigh what hangs upon the answer, for it is no small thing: nearly all we know of the heavens — the warmth of a distant star, the substance of a galaxy, the form of a virus too small to see — reaches us as light. A false account of light is a false account of all that rests upon it.
confucius-en.3: The Master said. This Area of Study — whose appointed question is How has understanding about the physical world changed? — advances in measured stages. Each stage sets down a settled model, brings forward one stubborn result of experiment, and follows the model patiently to its undoing. Figure 1.1 gathers the evidence in one view: stripes that grains cannot make, electrons cast out in a manner no wave can explain, and particles of matter that draw the patterns of waves. By the end, even space and time are revised.
confucius-en.4: Connects The study of Fields ended upon a great result: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field may give rise to one another, break free of every wire and charge, and travel onward through empty space — see Where the field idea is heading. This page takes up that result, raises it high, and then shows it cast down — not once, but twice.
confucius-en.5: In 1801 Thomas Young seemed to lay the long quarrel to rest. He let light pass through two narrow slits onto a screen. Were light made of Newton’s corpuscles, two bright stripes ought to appear, one behind each slit. Instead the screen showed many: a long and orderly procession of bright and dark bands, evenly spaced.
confucius-en.6: Only a wave will make such a thing. Where two ripples upon a pond meet crest upon crest, they swell together; crest upon trough, they cancel and lie flat. A bright band marks where the two slits’ waves arrive in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ — and a dark band marks a half-wavelength out of step, (n + ½)λ, just as sound and water waves behave when they overlap. So regular is the pattern that one formula, Δx = λL/d on formula sheet, binds the spacing of the fringes to the wavelength. With little more than a ruler, Young had thereby measured the wavelength of light.
confucius-en.7: A disciple asked: what, then, was waving? The answer came in the 1860s, drawn from what was already known. James Clerk Maxwell showed that a changing electric field begets a changing magnetic field, which begets the electric field again, and that this self-sustaining pair must travel — at a speed his equations fixed at 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, the very speed of light men had measured. Light may therefore be modelled as a transverse electromagnetic wave, born wherever charges accelerate; and every electromagnetic wave — from radio to gamma — travels at the same speed c through a vacuum.
confucius-en.8: One instance of this making you have already met. Electrons driven around the storage ring of the Australian Synchrotron are forever accelerating — for to move in a circle is to accelerate — and an accelerating charge gives off radiation. This is why the machine pours forth its light. By 1900, with Young’s pattern and Maxwell’s fields in accord, the matter seemed settled. Yet what seems settled is not always so.
confucius-en.9: Connects The wave model is set out in full in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the evidence of interference in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference. The fields herein are those charted in Unit 3.
confucius-en.10: The experiment that reopened the whole question looks, at first, a humble thing. Shine ultraviolet light upon a clean metal surface, and electrons are flung out of it — the photoelectric effect.
confucius-en.11: The wave model makes three predictions about it. Brighter light bears more energy, so the cast-out electrons should depart the faster. Any colour should serve, given time enough for the metal to gather energy. And in dim light there should be a pause while the energy slowly accumulates. So reasons the wave; but reason must bow to what is found.
confucius-en.12: Experiment gave the lie to every prediction. The greatest kinetic energy of the electrons depends only upon the light’s frequency; make the light brighter and you cast out more electrons, not swifter ones. Below a threshold frequency, none are cast out at all, however bright the light. And the casting-out is all but instant, even in the dimmest light.
confucius-en.13: The Master said. In 1905 Einstein offered an account the wave model could not hold: light delivers its energy in indivisible portions. Each portion — each photon — bears an energy given by E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck’s constant, equal to 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. One photon gives all its energy to one electron, which spends the metal’s exit toll — the work function φ — and keeps the rest as kinetic energy: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet. From this, each observation follows in order: frequency sets the size of each portion, brightness sets only their number, and a portion is given whole or not at all.
confucius-en.14: The way of measuring comes down from Unit 3. To learn the electrons’ energy, a reverse voltage is laid against them and raised until even the swiftest electron can no longer cross — the stopping voltage — which is W = qV not on sheet turned about, set as a brake rather than a spur. The electron-volt, that unit of energy named when charges are accelerated through potential differences, becomes the working coin of all that follows.
confucius-en.15: Connects The photon and the full account of the photoelectric effect are treated in chapter 14 · The photon; the stopping-voltage idea and the electron-volt were laid down in Electric fields · Work and energy.
confucius-en.16: In 1924 a French physicist, Louis de Broglie, put the question the other way about — the question that mirrors the first. If light, taken to be a wave, behaves as a stream of particles, then matter, taken to be particles, might behave as a wave. He proposed that every moving thing carries a wavelength, given by λ = h/p on formula sheet: the greater the momentum, the shorter the wave.
confucius-en.17: Three years later the trial was made: a beam of electrons was sent against a thin crystal. The beam came forth drawing rings, one within another, upon a screen — the very family of pattern that X-rays of equal wavelength make through the same crystal, as panel (c) of Figure 1.1 shows. The electron — that same particle hastened along by voltages and bent into circles by magnetic fields in Unit 3 — diffracts.
confucius-en.18: A disciple asked: why, then, does a cricket ball not diffract through a doorway? Because h is so very small. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ has a de Broglie wavelength of some 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m — far smaller than any gap it might pass through; and diffraction grows plain only when the wavelength is near the size of the opening. Electrons are light enough that their wavelengths match the spacing between atoms, and this is why crystals serve them as faithful diffraction gratings.
confucius-en.19: The Master said. The most fundamental form of the experiment is the one Simulation 1.1 shows. Electrons — or photons — are sent through Young’s double slits one at a time. Each lands upon the screen as a single small dot: a particle. Yet as thousands of dots gather, the interference fringes rise out of the tally: a wave. No single particle makes the pattern, yet every particle keeps faith with one.
confucius-en.20: Connects Matter waves are treated in chapter 15 · Matter waves. The electron beam shown there is the one built in Motors & accelerators — the same electrons, called now to a new office.
confucius-en.21: Two riddles about atoms went before these ideas. First, by the old reckoning atoms ought not to be stable at all: a classical electron circling a nucleus is accelerating, and an accelerating charge radiates — this is Maxwell’s own rule, the very one that accounts for the synchrotron’s light — so the electron should pour away its energy and spiral into the nucleus in a fraction of a second.
confucius-en.22: Second, glowing gases do not give a continuous spectrum. Each element gives only certain sharp colours — a line spectrum, peculiar to that element as a name is peculiar to a man. Sodium street lamps give their two yellow lines; metal-vapour lamps and neon signs each show their own; and sunlight bears thousands of dark lines, where cooler gas has taken away just those colours.
confucius-en.23: The explanation is quantisation. An electron bound within an atom may hold only certain energies — discrete levels, with nothing between. A fall from a higher level to a lower causes the atom to give off a single photon whose energy E = hf matches the gap exactly; and the taking-in of a photon of just a gap’s energy lifts the electron to a higher level. Bright emission lines and dark absorption lines answer to the one set of levels, travelled in opposite directions; thus a spectrum reveals the atom’s energy levels.
confucius-en.24: De Broglie’s wave, too, accounts for why the levels are discrete. When the electron’s wave is wrapped about the atom, it must close upon itself without break — a standing wave, like a string that will sound only its own harmonics. Only certain wavelengths fit the loop, and so only certain energies are allowed.
confucius-en.25: This description is a simplified model, refined by later physics, yet it accounts for the one feature the classical atom could not: why an atom has a lowest level — a ground state — beneath which the electron cannot fall, and therefore why atoms hold together at all. Quantisation belongs not to light alone; it lies at the root of matter.
confucius-en.26: Connects Spectra and energy levels are treated in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; the standing waves that beget the levels are introduced with light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
confucius-en.27: The Master said. One question lingered, left over from the wave model’s first triumph. Every wave known to physics waves within a medium — so men asked: what carries light across empty space? They named the proposed medium the aether, and in 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument fine enough to detect it: two light beams ran along arms set at right angles, met again to interfere, and the whole apparatus was turned and tried anew through the seasons as the Earth journeyed round the Sun. If the Earth moves through an aether, the beams’ times should differ; they never did — the most well-known null result in all physics.
confucius-en.28: In 1905 — the same year as the photon — Einstein took the result as it stood and rebuilt mechanics upon two postulates: that the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and that the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, heedless of the motion of the source or of the observer. The second postulate seems a small and harmless thing, but it is not. If every observer measures the same c, then time and distance themselves must differ from observer to observer.
confucius-en.29: The consequences bear names used throughout the year. Moving clocks run slow — time dilation, given by t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths shrink along the line of travel — length contraction, given by L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
confucius-en.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, stands only a hair above 1 at everyday speeds — which is why the effect went unseen for three centuries — and grows without bound as speed draws near c. The qualification “v << c” set upon Unit 3’s circular-path formula gave warning of this very condition, a whole unit before its time.
confucius-en.31: The evidence is read from the cosmic rays. Muons — heavy, short-lived kin of the electron, born kilometres overhead where cosmic rays strike — live on average about 2.2 μs. Even at 0.995c this allows but some 660 m of travel, yet muons reach the ground in great number. In the ground’s frame of reference their inner clocks run about ten times slow, so they endure long enough to finish the journey; in the muon’s own frame the atmosphere is contracted about tenfold, so the journey is short enough to finish — two tellings, one and the same outcome.
confucius-en.32: The satellites that find your phone carry clocks that run slow by a few microseconds each day on account of their orbital speed; left uncorrected, the positions they give would drift by kilometres each day. A phone applies special relativity every time it learns where it stands.
confucius-en.33: Connects The postulates, proper time and the muon evidence are developed in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. The “v << c” qualification was introduced upon the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.

The rest energy held within any object is given by E0 = mc² on formula sheet; and because the factor of conversion is c², a teaspoon of water holds rest energy enough to light twenty thousand homes for a year. confucius-en.36: The conversion runs both ways. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, both are undone, and two gamma-ray photons travel apart in opposite directions — opposite, because momentum must still keep its balance. Hospital PET scanners look within the body by catching exactly these pairs of photons.
confucius-en.37: In particle accelerators the conversion runs backward: the kinetic energy of a collision is turned into new particles that had no being before the impact. The synchrotron, studied earlier as a mere exercise in circular motion, is more truly a machine whose every magnet is wrought about γ.
confucius-en.38: Connects The full account of energy is developed in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that furnish that energy were treated in Motors & accelerators.
confucius-en.39: The Master said. The whole Area of Study has now been read through once: a wave that proved to arrive in discrete portions, particles that proved to interfere, atoms accounted for by standing waves, and a speed limit that bends time and length and names mass a gathered form of energy. curie.0: Unit 4 Area of Study 1 concerns three established models — light as a wave, matter as particles, and space and time as fixed — and the experiments that overturned each of them. I intend it to be read as a single overview. Nothing here requires solving.
curie.1: The question that organises the whole of this page is the nature of light. Newton held that it was a stream of tiny particles, which he termed corpuscles. His contemporary Huygens held that it was a wave. The dispute continued for more than a century, because both models accounted for the everyday evidence of reflection, refraction and shadows.
curie.2: The implications are substantial: almost all knowledge of the universe — the temperature of stars, the composition of galaxies, the structure of a virus — arrives as light. An incorrect account of light entails an incorrect account of everything that depends on it.
curie.3: This Area of Study — its official question is How has understanding about the physical world changed? — proceeds in stages. Each stage opens with an established model, introduces one persistent experimental result, and follows the model to its failure. Figure 1.1 records the evidence: stripes that particles cannot produce, electrons ejected in a manner waves cannot explain, and particles of matter producing wave patterns. By the conclusion, space and time themselves are also revised.
curie.4: Connects The Fields material concluded with a key result: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field can regenerate each other, detach from every wire and charge, and travel through empty space — see Where the field idea is heading. This page develops that result, and then shows it overturned, twice.
curie.5: In 1801 Thomas Young appeared to settle the long-standing dispute. He passed light through two narrow slits onto a screen. Were light composed of Newton’s corpuscles, two bright stripes should appear, one behind each slit. Instead the screen showed many: a long series of evenly spaced bright and dark bands.
curie.6: Only a wave produces this result. Where two ripples on a pond meet crest on crest, they reinforce; crest on trough, they cancel. A bright band marks a position where the waves from the two slits arrive in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ — and a dark band marks a half-wavelength of mismatch, (n + ½)λ, in the same manner as overlapping sound and water waves. The pattern is sufficiently regular that one formula, Δx = λL/d on formula sheet, relates the fringe spacing to the wavelength. Young had thereby measured the wavelength of light with little more than a ruler.
curie.7: What was waving was answered in the 1860s from material already established. James Clerk Maxwell showed that a changing electric field creates a changing magnetic field, which recreates the electric field, and that this self-sustaining pair must travel — at a speed his equations fixed at 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, the measured speed of light. Light can therefore be modelled as a transverse electromagnetic wave, produced wherever charges accelerate, and every electromagnetic wave — radio to gamma — travels at the same speed c in a vacuum.
curie.8: A practical example of this production has already been encountered. Electrons forced around the Australian Synchrotron’s storage ring are continuously accelerating — circular motion is acceleration — and accelerating charges radiate. That accounts for the machine’s emission of light. By 1900, with Young’s pattern and Maxwell’s fields in agreement, the matter appeared resolved.
curie.9: Connects The wave model is developed fully in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the interference evidence in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference. The fields involved are those mapped in Unit 3.
curie.10: The experiment that reopened the matter appears unremarkable. When ultraviolet light is directed onto a clean metal surface, electrons are ejected from it — the photoelectric effect.
curie.11: The wave model makes three predictions about it. Brighter light carries more energy, so the ejected electrons should leave faster. Any colour should be effective, given time for the metal to absorb energy. And in dim light there should be a delay while the energy accumulates.
curie.12: Experiment contradicted each prediction. The maximum kinetic energy of the electrons depends only on the light’s frequency; increasing the brightness ejects more electrons, not faster ones. Below a threshold frequency, no electrons are emitted at all, at any brightness. And emission is effectively instantaneous, even in the dimmest light.
curie.13: In 1905 Einstein proposed an account the wave model could not accommodate: light delivers its energy in indivisible amounts. Each amount — each photon — carries an energy given by E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck’s constant, equal to 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. One photon transfers all its energy to one electron, which expends the metal’s exit energy — the work function φ — and retains the remainder as kinetic energy: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet. Each observation follows from this: frequency sets the energy of each amount, brightness sets only the number of amounts, and an amount is transferred whole or not at all.
curie.14: The measuring method derives from Unit 3. To determine the electrons’ energy, a reverse voltage is applied and increased until even the fastest electron no longer crosses — the stopping voltage — which is W = qV not on sheet applied in reverse, as a brake rather than an accelerator. The electron-volt, the energy unit defined when charges are accelerated through potential differences, becomes the working unit of everything that follows.
curie.15: Connects The photon and the full photoelectric analysis are treated in chapter 14 · The photon; the stopping-voltage idea and the electron-volt were established in Electric fields · Work and energy.
curie.16: In 1924 a French physicist, Louis de Broglie, posed the symmetric question. If light — taken to be a wave — behaves as a stream of particles, matter — taken to be particles — might behave as a wave. He proposed that every moving object carries a wavelength, given by λ = h/p on formula sheet: the larger the momentum, the shorter the wave.
curie.17: Three years later the test was conducted: a beam of electrons was fired at a thin crystal. The beam emerged producing concentric rings on a screen — the same family of pattern that X-rays of equal wavelength produce through the same crystal, as panel (c) of Figure 1.1 shows. The electron, the particle accelerated with voltages and steered into circular paths with magnetic fields in Unit 3, diffracts.
curie.18: A cricket ball does not diffract through a doorway because h is so small. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ has a de Broglie wavelength of about 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m — far smaller than any gap it could pass through, and diffraction becomes noticeable only when the wavelength is comparable to the opening. Electrons are sufficiently light that their wavelengths match the spacing between atoms, which is why crystals act as effective diffraction gratings for them.
curie.19: The most fundamental version of the experiment is the one Simulation 1.1 demonstrates. Electrons — or photons — are sent through Young’s double slits one at a time. Each arrives at the screen as a single localised dot: a particle. But as thousands of dots accumulate, the interference fringes form out of the statistics: a wave. No single particle produces a pattern, yet every particle conforms to one.
curie.20: Connects Matter waves are treated in chapter 15 · Matter waves. The electron beam featured there is the one constructed in Motors & accelerators — the same electrons in a new role.
curie.21: Two unresolved facts about atoms preceded these ideas. First, on the classical account atoms should not be stable: a classical electron circling a nucleus is accelerating, and accelerating charges radiate — Maxwell’s own rule, the one that accounts for the synchrotron’s emission — so the electron should radiate its energy away and spiral into the nucleus in a fraction of a second.
curie.22: Second, glowing gases do not emit a continuous spectrum. Each element emits only certain sharp colours — a line spectrum, distinctive to that element. Sodium street lamps emit their two yellow lines; metal vapour lamps and neon signs each show their own line spectrum; sunlight carries thousands of dark lines where cooler gas has removed exactly those colours.
curie.23: The explanation is quantisation. An electron bound in an atom can hold only certain energies — discrete levels, with none in between. A transition from a higher level to a lower one causes the atom to emit a single photon whose energy E = hf exactly matches the gap; absorption of a photon of exactly a gap’s energy causes the electron to move to a higher level. Bright emission lines and dark absorption lines correspond to the same set of levels in opposite directions, and a spectrum thereby indicates the atom’s energy levels.
curie.24: De Broglie’s wave also accounts for the existence of discrete levels. When the electron’s wave is wrapped around the atom, it must close on itself smoothly — a standing wave, comparable to a string that sounds only its harmonics. Only certain wavelengths fit the loop, so only certain energies are permitted.
curie.25: The description is a simplified model, refined by later physics, but it accounts for the one feature the classical atom could not: why an atom has a lowest level — a ground state — below which the electron cannot fall, and therefore why atoms are stable. Quantisation is not confined to light; it is fundamental to matter.
curie.26: Connects Spectra and energy levels are treated in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; the standing waves that produce the levels are introduced with light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
curie.27: One unresolved question remained from the wave model’s first success. Every wave known to physics waves in a medium — so the question arose of what carries light across empty space. Physicists named the proposed medium the aether, and in 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument precise enough to detect it: two light beams travelled along perpendicular arms, recombined to interfere, and the whole apparatus was rotated and re-tested through the seasons as Earth moved around the Sun. If Earth moves through an aether, the beam times should differ; they never did — the most well-known null result in physics.
curie.28: In 1905 — the same year as the photon — Einstein accepted the result as it stood and reconstructed mechanics from two postulates: the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, regardless of the motion of the source or the observer. The second postulate appears unremarkable but is not. If every observer measures the same c, then time and distance themselves must differ between observers.
curie.29: The consequences carry names used throughout the year. Moving clocks run slow — time dilation, given by t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths contract along the direction of travel — length contraction, given by L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
curie.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, is only marginally above 1 at everyday speeds — which is why the effect was not detected for three centuries — and grows without limit as speed approaches c. The qualification “v << c” attached to Unit 3’s circular-path formula indicated this condition, stated a unit in advance.
curie.31: The evidence is observed from cosmic rays. Muons — heavy, short-lived relatives of the electron, created kilometres overhead by cosmic rays — have an average lifetime of about 2.2 μs. Even at 0.995c this permits roughly 660 m of travel, yet muons reach the ground in large numbers. In the ground frame of reference their internal clocks run about ten times slow, so they survive long enough to complete the journey; in the muon’s frame of reference the atmosphere is contracted about tenfold, so the journey is short enough to complete — two descriptions, one consistent outcome.
curie.32: The satellites used to locate a phone carry clocks that run slow by a few microseconds each day because of their orbital speed; uncorrected, GPS positions would drift by kilometres each day. A phone applies special relativity each time it determines a location.
curie.33: Connects Postulates, proper time and the muon evidence are developed in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. The “v << c” qualification was introduced on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
curie.34: Einstein’s relativity papers of 1905 concluded with a brief result that proved more significant than the rest: mass is a concentrated form of energy.
