curie.35: The Sun operates on this conversion. In its core, hydrogen fuses into helium, and the products have slightly less mass than the reactants; the missing mass is emitted as light. The Sun converts about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiated energy each second — sunlight is the Sun’s mass, converted at the universal rate.
curie.36: The conversion operates in both directions. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, both are annihilated and two gamma-ray photons travel apart in opposite directions — opposite, because momentum must still balance. Hospital PET scanners image the body by detecting exactly these photon pairs.
curie.37: In particle accelerators the conversion proceeds in reverse: the kinetic energy of a collision is converted into new particles that did not exist before the impact. The synchrotron studied earlier as a circular-motion exercise is, more precisely, a machine whose every magnet is engineered around γ.
curie.38: Connects The full energy account is developed in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that supply that energy were treated in Motors & accelerators.
curie.39: The whole Area of Study has now been read once: a wave that proved to arrive in discrete amounts, particles that proved to interfere, atoms accounted for by standing waves, and a speed limit that alters time and length and identifies mass as concentrated energy. What follows is the same material at greater depth.

mack.0: Unit 4 Area of Study 1 is the story of three rock-solid models — light as a wave, matter as particles, space and time as fixed — and the experiments that knocked each one off its pedestal. Read it straight through, as one big picture. There’s nothing here to solve.
mack.1: One question runs through this entire page: what, exactly, is light? Newton was sure it was a stream of tiny particles, which he called corpuscles. His contemporary Huygens was equally sure it was a wave. The argument dragged on for over a century — and the maddening part is that both models handled the everyday evidence of reflection, refraction and shadows just fine.
mack.2: And the stakes are huge: almost everything we know about the universe — the temperature of stars, what galaxies are made of, the structure of a virus — arrives as light. Get light wrong and you’ve got everything that rides on it wrong too.
mack.3: This Area of Study — its official question is How has understanding about the physical world changed? — unfolds in stages. Each stage opens with a model everyone trusted, drops in one stubborn experimental result, and follows the model all the way to where it breaks. Figure 1.1 lays out the evidence: stripes that particles simply cannot make, electrons flung out in a way no wave can explain, and lumps of matter producing wave patterns. By the end, even space and time get rewritten.
mack.4: Connects The Fields material ended on a striking result: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field can keep regenerating each other, peel away from every wire and charge, and sail off through empty space — see Where the field idea is heading. This page takes that result and runs with it — then watches it get overturned, twice.
mack.5: In 1801 Thomas Young looked to have settled the whole argument. He sent light through two narrow slits onto a screen. If light were Newton’s corpuscles, you’d expect two bright stripes, one behind each slit. Instead the screen lit up with many of them: a long, evenly spaced row of bright and dark bands.
mack.6: Only a wave does that. Where two ripples on a pond meet crest on crest, they pile up and reinforce; crest on trough, they wipe each other out. A bright band marks a spot where the waves from the two slits arrive in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ — and a dark band marks a half-wavelength of mismatch, (n + ½)λ, exactly the way overlapping sound and water waves behave. The pattern is so tidy that a single formula, Δx = λL/d on formula sheet, links the fringe spacing to the wavelength. Just like that, Young had measured the wavelength of light with little more than a ruler.
mack.7: What was actually doing the waving got answered in the 1860s, using physics already on the books. James Clerk Maxwell showed that a changing electric field makes a changing magnetic field, which remakes the electric field, and that this self-sustaining pair has no choice but to travel — at a speed his equations pinned to 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, the measured speed of light. So light can be modelled as a transverse electromagnetic wave, thrown off wherever charges accelerate, and every electromagnetic wave — radio right up to gamma — travels at the same speed c in a vacuum.
mack.8: You’ve already met this kind of light-production in action. Electrons driven around the Australian Synchrotron’s storage ring are accelerating non-stop — circular motion is acceleration — and accelerating charges radiate. That’s why the machine pours out light. By 1900, with Young’s pattern and Maxwell’s fields singing in harmony, the case looked closed.
mack.9: Connects The wave model gets the full treatment in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the interference evidence in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference. The fields in play are the ones mapped out in Unit 3.
mack.10: The experiment that cracked the case back open looks almost boring. Shine ultraviolet light onto a clean metal surface and electrons come flying off it — the photoelectric effect.
mack.11: The wave model makes three confident predictions about it. Brighter light carries more energy, so the ejected electrons should leave faster. Any colour should work, given the metal enough time to soak up the energy. And in dim light there should be a wait while the energy piles up.
mack.12: Experiment shot down every one. The maximum kinetic energy of the electrons depends only on the light’s frequency; crank up the brightness and you eject more electrons, not faster ones. Below a threshold frequency, no electrons come off at all, no matter how bright. And the emission is basically instant, even in the faintest light.
mack.13: In 1905 Einstein floated an idea the wave model just couldn’t swallow: light hands over its energy in indivisible chunks. Each chunk — each photon — carries an energy set by E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck’s constant, equal to 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. One photon dumps all its energy into one electron, which spends the metal’s exit fee — the work function φ — and keeps whatever’s left as kinetic energy: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet. Every observation falls right out of this: frequency sets the energy of each chunk, brightness sets only the number of chunks, and a chunk is delivered whole or not at all.
mack.14: The way you measure it comes straight from Unit 3. To pin down the electrons’ energy, you apply a reverse voltage and keep cranking it up until even the fastest electron can no longer make it across — the stopping voltage — which is just W = qV not on sheet run in reverse, a brake instead of an accelerator. The electron-volt, that energy unit defined when charges are pushed through potential differences, becomes the working currency of everything that follows.
mack.15: Connects The photon and the full photoelectric analysis are handled in chapter 14 · The photon; the stopping-voltage idea and the electron-volt were set up in Electric fields · Work and energy.
mack.16: In 1924 a French physicist, Louis de Broglie, asked the mirror-image question. If light — supposedly a wave — behaves like a stream of particles, then maybe matter — supposedly particles — behaves like a wave. He proposed that every moving object carries a wavelength, given by λ = h/p on formula sheet: the bigger the momentum, the shorter the wave.
mack.17: Three years later they put it to the test: fire a beam of electrons at a thin crystal. The beam came out making concentric rings on a screen — the very same family of pattern that X-rays of equal wavelength make through the same crystal, as panel (c) of Figure 1.1 shows. The electron — the particle you accelerated with voltages and steered into circular paths with magnetic fields in Unit 3 — diffracts.
mack.18: A cricket ball doesn’t diffract through a doorway because h is so tiny. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ has a de Broglie wavelength of roughly 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m — far, far smaller than any gap it could squeeze through, and diffraction only becomes noticeable when the wavelength is comparable to the opening. Electrons are light enough that their wavelengths match the spacing between atoms, which is exactly why crystals make such good diffraction gratings for them.
mack.19: The most stripped-down version of the experiment is the one Simulation 1.1 shows off. Electrons — or photons — go through Young’s double slits one at a time. Each one lands on the screen as a single pinpoint dot: a particle. But as thousands of dots stack up, the interference fringes emerge out of the statistics: a wave. No single particle makes a pattern, yet every single particle obeys one.
mack.20: Connects Matter waves are handled in chapter 15 · Matter waves. The electron beam starring there is the one built back in Motors & accelerators — the same electrons, cast in a new role.
mack.21: Two nagging facts about atoms were sitting around before any of these ideas turned up. First, on the classical story atoms shouldn’t even be stable: a classical electron circling a nucleus is accelerating, and accelerating charges radiate — Maxwell’s own rule, the very one that explains the synchrotron’s glow — so the electron ought to radiate its energy away and spiral into the nucleus in a fraction of a second.
mack.22: Second, glowing gases don’t pour out a smooth, continuous spectrum. Each element emits only certain sharp colours — a line spectrum, a fingerprint unique to that element. Sodium street lamps give off their two yellow lines; metal vapour lamps and neon signs each flash their own line spectrum; sunlight comes laced with thousands of dark lines where cooler gas has stripped out exactly those colours.
mack.23: The explanation is quantisation. An electron bound in an atom can hold only certain energies — discrete levels, with nothing in between. Drop from a higher level to a lower one and the atom spits out a single photon whose energy E = hf matches the gap exactly; soak up a photon of exactly a gap’s energy and the electron climbs to a higher level. Bright emission lines and dark absorption lines are the same set of levels read in opposite directions, so a spectrum is really a readout of the atom’s energy levels.
mack.24: De Broglie’s wave also explains why those discrete levels exist in the first place. Wrap the electron’s wave around the atom and it has to close on itself smoothly — a standing wave, just like a string that only sounds its harmonics. Only certain wavelengths fit the loop, so only certain energies are allowed.
mack.25: It’s a simplified picture, tidied up by later physics, but it nails the one thing the classical atom never could: why an atom has a lowest rung — a ground state — below which the electron can’t fall, and so why atoms are stable at all. Quantisation isn’t just a light thing; it’s built into matter.
mack.26: Connects Spectra and energy levels are handled in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; the standing waves that produce those levels first show up alongside light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
mack.27: One loose end was still dangling from the wave model’s first triumph. Every wave physics knew of waves in something — so what on earth carries light across empty space? Physicists named the proposed medium the aether, and in 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument delicate enough to hunt for it: two light beams ran down perpendicular arms, recombined to interfere, and the whole apparatus was spun around and re-tested through the seasons as Earth swung around the Sun. If Earth ploughs through an aether, the beam times should differ; they never did — the most famous null result in physics.
mack.28: In 1905 — the same year as the photon — Einstein took the result at face value and rebuilt mechanics from two postulates: the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, no matter how the source or the observer is moving. The second one sounds harmless and absolutely is not. If every observer measures the same c, then time and distance themselves have to differ between observers.
mack.29: The consequences come with names you’ll hear all year. Moving clocks run slow — time dilation, given by t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths shrink along the direction of travel — length contraction, given by L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
mack.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, sits barely above 1 at everyday speeds — which is exactly why nobody noticed the effect for three centuries — and then blows up without limit as speed creeps toward c. That little "v << c" stuck onto Unit 3’s circular-path formula was quietly flagging this very condition, a whole unit ahead of time.
mack.31: The evidence rains down from cosmic rays. Muons — heavy, short-lived cousins of the electron, born kilometres overhead when cosmic rays hit the atmosphere — live on average about 2.2 μs. Even at 0.995c that should only get them roughly 660 m, yet muons reach the ground in droves. In the ground frame of reference their internal clocks tick about ten times slow, so they last long enough to finish the trip; in the muon’s own frame of reference the atmosphere is squashed about tenfold, so the trip is short enough to finish — two stories, one matching outcome.
mack.32: The satellites that figure out where your phone is carry clocks that run slow by a few microseconds each day thanks to their orbital speed; left uncorrected, GPS positions would drift off by kilometres every day. Your phone is quietly doing special relativity every single time it finds your location.
mack.33: Connects Postulates, proper time and the muon evidence are developed in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. The "v << c" qualification was first introduced on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
mack.34: Einstein’s 1905 relativity papers signed off with a short result that turned out to outshine everything around it: mass is just a concentrated stash of energy. The rest energy locked inside any object is given by E0 = mc² on formula sheet, and because the conversion factor is c², a teaspoon of water holds enough rest energy to power twenty thousand homes with electricity for a year. Set the object moving and its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, with the kinetic slice given by Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet — and at everyday speeds this collapses back to the familiar formula, one more approximation whose fine print now makes perfect sense.
mack.35: The Sun runs on exactly this conversion. In its core, hydrogen fuses into helium, and the products weigh slightly less than the reactants; the missing mass departs as light. The Sun turns about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiated energy every second — sunlight is the Sun’s own mass, cashed in at the universal rate.
mack.36: The conversion runs both ways. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, both vanish and two gamma-ray photons fly apart in opposite directions — opposite, because momentum still has to balance. Hospital PET scanners image the body by catching exactly these photon pairs.
mack.37: In particle accelerators the conversion goes in reverse: the kinetic energy of a collision gets converted into brand-new particles that didn’t exist before the smash. The synchrotron you studied earlier as a circular-motion exercise is, more precisely, a machine with every single magnet engineered around γ.
mack.38: Connects The full energy account is developed in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that supply that energy were treated in Motors & accelerators.
mack.39: That’s the whole Area of Study, read once through: a wave that turned out to arrive in discrete chunks, particles that turned out to interfere, atoms explained by standing waves, and a speed limit that warps time and length and reveals mass as bottled-up energy. What comes next is all the same material, dug into far deeper.
mack.40: Pass 2 develops it across six chapters, numbered 6 to 11, each one meant to be read the night before class hits that topic: light as a wave; diffraction and interference; the photon; matter waves; atoms and spectra; relativity and mass–energy. Each carries every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and checks to make sure each idea has actually landed.
mack.41: Pass 3 is your exam-prep gear. Four pages cover the twenty-eight key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — light as a wave; light and matter as particles; light–matter similarities; relativity and mass–energy — with the subtleties, the classic mistakes, the assessors’ advice and exam-style questions with full solutions. The depth row below takes you downward whenever you need it, and this page stays one click away for whenever the detail starts swallowing the big picture.

So light can be thought of as a transverse electromagnetic wave, thrown off wherever charges accelerate, and every electromagnetic wave — radio through gamma — rips along at the same speed c in a vacuum.
czerski.8: You’ve already met this kind of light-making in action. Electrons whipped around the Australian Synchrotron’s storage ring are accelerating the whole time — going in a circle is accelerating — and accelerating charges throw off radiation. That’s where the machine’s light comes from. By 1900, with Young’s pattern and Maxwell’s fields singing the same tune, the case looked closed.
czerski.9: Connects The wave model gets the full treatment in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the interference evidence in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference. The fields in play are the ones mapped out in Unit 3.
czerski.10: The experiment that cracked the case back open looks almost dull. Shine ultraviolet light onto a clean metal surface and electrons come flying off it — the photoelectric effect.
czerski.11: The wave model makes three confident predictions about it. Brighter light carries more energy, so the electrons should leave faster. Any colour ought to do the job, given the metal a moment to soak up energy. And in dim light there should be a pause while the energy builds up.
czerski.12: Every single prediction fell flat. The fastest the electrons leave depends only on the light’s frequency; crank up the brightness and you get more electrons, not quicker ones. Below a threshold frequency, nothing comes off at all, no matter how bright the light. And the electrons appear the instant the light arrives, even in the faintest glow.
czerski.13: In 1905 Einstein offered an explanation the wave model just couldn’t stomach: light hands over its energy in indivisible little packets. Each packet — each photon — carries an energy given by E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck’s constant, equal to 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. One photon dumps all its energy into one electron, which pays the metal’s exit fee — the work function φ — and keeps whatever’s left over as kinetic energy: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet. Every one of those puzzling observations falls straight out of this: frequency fixes the energy of each packet, brightness fixes only how many packets there are, and a packet is handed over whole or not at all.
czerski.14: The way you actually measure this comes straight from Unit 3. To find out how much energy the electrons carry, you push back against them with a reverse voltage and turn it up until even the fastest one can’t make it across — the stopping voltage — which is just W = qV not on sheet run backwards, as a brake instead of an accelerator. The electron-volt, the energy unit you met when charges get pushed through potential differences, becomes the everyday currency of everything that follows.
czerski.15: Connects The photon and the full photoelectric story are worked through in chapter 14 · The photon; the stopping-voltage idea and the electron-volt were set up in Electric fields · Work and energy.
czerski.16: In 1924 a French physicist, Louis de Broglie, turned the question on its head. If light — supposedly a wave — can act like a stream of particles, then maybe matter — supposedly particles — can act like a wave. He suggested that every moving thing drags a wavelength along with it, given by λ = h/p on formula sheet: the more momentum, the shorter the wave.
czerski.17: Three years on, the test was run: a beam of electrons fired at a thin crystal. Out the far side came concentric rings on a screen — the very same family of pattern that X-rays of matching wavelength make going through the same crystal, as panel (c) of Figure 1.1 shows. The electron — the same particle you accelerated with voltages and curved around with magnetic fields back in Unit 3 — diffracts.
czerski.18: A cricket ball doesn’t diffract through a doorway, and that’s purely because h is so tiny. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ has a de Broglie wavelength of about 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m — vastly smaller than any gap it could ever squeeze through, and diffraction only shows up when the wavelength is roughly the size of the opening. Electrons are light enough that their wavelengths match the gaps between atoms, which is exactly why a crystal works as a diffraction grating for them.
czerski.19: The cleanest, most stripped-down version of all this is the one Simulation 1.1 shows you. Electrons — or photons — go through Young’s double slits one at a time. Each one lands on the screen as a single sharp dot: a particle, plainly. But let thousands of dots pile up and the interference fringes grow out of the tally: a wave. No single particle makes a pattern, yet every last one obeys it.
czerski.20: Connects Matter waves are covered in chapter 15 · Matter waves. The electron beam there is the very one built in Motors & accelerators — the same electrons, doing a new job.
czerski.21: Two loose ends about atoms were already dangling before any of these ideas turned up. First, on the classical story an atom shouldn’t even hold together: a classical electron looping a nucleus is accelerating, and accelerating charges radiate — Maxwell’s own rule, the one behind the synchrotron’s glow — so the electron ought to bleed its energy away and spiral into the nucleus in a fraction of a second.
czerski.22: Second, glowing gases don’t pour out a smooth rainbow. Each element gives off only certain sharp colours — a line spectrum, as distinctive as a fingerprint. Sodium street lamps put out their two yellow lines; metal-vapour lamps and neon signs each flash their own line spectrum; sunlight comes laced with thousands of dark lines where cooler gas has stripped out exactly those colours.
czerski.23: The answer is quantisation. An electron locked inside an atom can only hold certain energies — separate, discrete rungs, with nothing allowed in between. Drop from a higher rung to a lower one and the atom spits out a single photon whose energy E = hf matches the gap exactly; swallow a photon of precisely a gap’s worth of energy and the electron climbs to a higher rung. Bright emission lines and dark absorption lines are the same set of rungs read in opposite directions, so a spectrum is really a read-out of the atom’s energy levels.
czerski.24: De Broglie’s wave even explains why those rungs exist at all. Wrap the electron’s wave around the atom and it has to join up smoothly with itself — a standing wave, just like a guitar string that’ll only ring at its harmonics. Only certain wavelengths fit neatly round the loop, so only certain energies are allowed.
czerski.25: It’s a simplified picture, tidied up by later physics, but it nails the one thing the classical atom never could: why an atom has a lowest rung — a ground state — that the electron can’t fall below, and so why atoms hold together at all. Quantisation isn’t just a quirk of light; it’s woven into matter itself.
czerski.26: Connects Spectra and energy levels are covered in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; the standing waves that set up the levels first appear alongside light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
czerski.27: One question was still nagging away from the wave model’s first triumph. Every wave physics knew about waved in something — so what on earth carries light across empty space? Physicists named the supposed stuff the aether, and in 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument fine enough to catch it: two light beams ran down arms at right angles, met up again to interfere, and the whole rig was spun round and re-checked through the seasons as Earth swung round the Sun. If Earth were sliding through an aether, the two beam times should come out different; they never did — the most famous null result in physics.
czerski.28: In 1905 — the same year as the photon — Einstein took the result at face value and rebuilt mechanics from two postulates: the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, no matter how the source or the observer happens to be moving. The second one sounds harmless and is anything but. If everybody measures the same c, then time and distance themselves have to come out different for different observers.
czerski.29: The consequences have names you’ll hear all year. Moving clocks run slow — time dilation, given by t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths shrink along the direction they’re travelling — length contraction, given by L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
czerski.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, sits only a whisker above 1 at everyday speeds — which is exactly why nobody spotted the effect for three centuries — and then shoots up without limit as speed creeps toward c. That “v << c” tag stuck onto Unit 3’s circular-path formula was flagging this very condition, a whole unit early.
czerski.31: The proof rains down from cosmic rays. Muons — heavy, short-lived cousins of the electron, made kilometres up by cosmic rays — live on average about 2.2 μs. Even at 0.995c that’s only enough for roughly 660 m of travel, yet muons reach the ground in droves. In the ground frame of reference their internal clocks tick about ten times slow, so they last long enough to finish the trip; in the muon’s own frame of reference the atmosphere is squashed about tenfold, so the trip is short enough to finish — two stories, one matching ending.
czerski.32: The satellites that pin down your phone carry clocks that run slow by a few microseconds a day thanks to their orbital speed; left uncorrected, GPS fixes would wander off by kilometres a day. Your phone does special relativity every single time it works out where you are.
czerski.33: Connects The postulates, proper time and the muon evidence get developed in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. The “v << c” tag first showed up on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
czerski.34: Einstein’s relativity papers of 1905 ended on a short result that turned out to matter more than everything before it: mass is just energy, packed tight. The rest energy locked inside any object is given by E0 = mc² on formula sheet, and because the conversion rate is c², a teaspoon of water holds enough rest energy to keep twenty thousand homes in electricity for a year. Set the object moving and its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, with the kinetic slice given by Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet — and at everyday speeds this folds back into the familiar formula, one more approximation whose small print you can now read.
czerski.35: The Sun runs on exactly this trade. Down in its core, hydrogen fuses into helium, and the products weigh a touch less than the ingredients; the missing mass leaves as light. The Sun turns about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiated energy every second — sunlight is the Sun’s own mass, cashed in at the universal rate.
czerski.36: The trade runs both ways. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, the two wipe each other out and two gamma-ray photons shoot off back-to-back — back-to-back, because momentum still has to balance. Hospital PET scanners picture the body by catching exactly these photon pairs.
czerski.37: In particle accelerators the trade runs in reverse: the kinetic energy of a smash-up is turned into brand-new particles that didn’t exist before the collision. The synchrotron you met earlier as a tidy circular-motion exercise is, more honestly, a machine with every magnet engineered around γ.
czerski.38: Connects The full energy account is developed in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that pour in that energy were covered in Motors & accelerators.
czerski.39: And that’s the whole Area of Study read through once: a wave that turned out to arrive in discrete packets, particles that turned out to interfere, atoms explained by standing waves, and a speed limit that bends time and length and reveals mass as energy in concentrated form. What comes next is the same material, gone into properly.
czerski.40: Pass 2 builds it out across six chapters, numbered 6 to 11, each one meant to be read the night before class hits the topic: light as a wave; diffraction and interference; the photon; matter waves; atoms and spectra; relativity and mass–energy. Each carries every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and quick checks to make sure each idea has actually landed.
czerski.41: Pass 3 is your exam-prep gear. Four pages cover the twenty-eight key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — light as a wave; light and matter as particles; light–matter similarities; relativity and mass–energy — with the subtleties, the usual slip-ups, the assessors’ advice and exam-style questions with full solutions. The depth row below takes you down whenever you need it, and this page stays one click away for whenever the detail starts hiding the big picture.

sagan.0: Unit 4 Area of Study 1 is the story of three great certainties — that light is a wave, that matter is made of particles, that space and time stand fixed and absolute — and of the experiments that, one by one, swept each of them away. Read it as a single sweep of the eye across the whole vista. Nothing here asks to be solved; it asks only to be wondered at.
sagan.1: One question presides over this entire page: what, in truth, is light? Newton was convinced it was a hail of tiny grains, which he called corpuscles. His contemporary Huygens was just as convinced it was a wave. The quarrel ran on for more than a hundred years, because both pictures could honour the everyday testimony of reflection, refraction and shadow.
sagan.2: And the stakes could hardly be higher: nearly everything we know of the universe — the temperature of a distant star, the makeup of a galaxy, the very architecture of a virus — comes to us riding on light. Get light wrong, and we get wrong every single thing that rests upon it.
sagan.3: This Area of Study — its official question is How has understanding about the physical world changed? — unfolds in stages. Each stage begins with a settled, confident model, brings forward one stubborn experimental result that will not go away, and follows the model patiently to the moment it breaks. Figure 1.1 gathers the evidence: stripes that no rain of particles could ever paint, electrons flung off in a way no wave could account for, and particles of solid matter tracing out the rings of a wave. By the end, even space and time themselves are remade.
sagan.4: Connects The Fields material ended on a remarkable note: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field can give birth to each other, tear free of every wire and charge that made them, and journey on through the emptiness alone — see Where the field idea is heading. This page takes up that result, carries it forward, and then watches it overturned — not once, but twice.
sagan.5: In 1801 Thomas Young seemed at last to put the old dispute to rest. He sent light through two narrow slits onto a screen. If light were truly Newton's hail of corpuscles, two bright stripes should fall there, one behind each slit, and nothing more. Instead the screen blossomed with many: a long, patient procession of evenly spaced bright and dark bands.
sagan.6: Only a wave can do this. Where two ripples spreading across a pond meet crest upon crest, they swell together; crest upon trough, they erase one another into stillness. A bright band marks a place where the waves from the two slits arrive perfectly in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ — and a dark band marks a half-wavelength of disagreement, (n + ½)λ, exactly as overlapping sound and water waves behave. The pattern is so faithful, so regular, that a single formula, Δx = λL/d on formula sheet, ties the spacing of the fringes to the wavelength itself. With little more than a ruler, Young had measured the wavelength of light.
sagan.7: What, then, was waving? The answer arrived in the 1860s, drawn from ground already won. James Clerk Maxwell showed that a changing electric field conjures a changing magnetic field, which conjures the electric field anew, and that this self-sustaining pair has no choice but to travel — at a speed his equations pinned down at 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, the very speed of light men had measured. Light, then, can be understood as a transverse electromagnetic wave, born wherever charges accelerate; and every electromagnetic wave there is — from the longest radio swell to the fiercest gamma ray — races at that one same speed c through the vacuum.
sagan.8: We have already met one of these waves being made. The electrons driven around the storage ring of the Australian Synchrotron are forever accelerating — for circular motion is acceleration — and accelerating charges shed light. That is why the great machine glows. By 1900, with Young's pattern and Maxwell's fields singing in agreement, the question of light seemed forever closed.
sagan.9: Connects The wave model is unfolded in full in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the evidence of interference in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference. The fields at work here are the very ones charted in Unit 3.
sagan.10: The experiment that pried the question open again looks, at first glance, almost trivial. Shine ultraviolet light onto a clean metal surface, and electrons leap out of it — the photoelectric effect.
sagan.11: The wave model makes three confident predictions about it. Brighter light carries more energy, so the escaping electrons ought to leave faster. Any colour should serve, given only enough time for the metal to drink in the energy. And in dim light there should be a pause, a patient gathering, while the energy slowly accumulates.
sagan.12: Experiment overturned every one. The greatest kinetic energy of the electrons depends on one thing alone — the frequency of the light; turning up the brightness sends out more electrons, never faster ones. Below a certain threshold frequency, not a single electron emerges, however blinding the glare. And the emission is all but instantaneous, even in the faintest light imaginable.
sagan.13: In 1905 Einstein offered an account the wave model simply could not hold: light hands over its energy in indivisible parcels. Each parcel — each photon — carries an energy given by E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck's constant, equal to 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. One photon pours all its energy into one electron, which pays the metal's price of escape — the work function φ — and keeps whatever is left as kinetic energy: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet. Every observation falls into place: frequency sets the energy of each parcel, brightness sets only how many parcels, and a parcel is delivered whole or not at all.
sagan.14: The way we measure all this comes straight from Unit 3. To weigh the electrons' energy, a reverse voltage is raised higher and higher until even the swiftest electron can no longer cross — the stopping voltage — which is simply W = qV not on sheet turned around, a brake instead of an accelerator. And the electron-volt, that unit of energy born when charges fall through potential differences, becomes the working coin of everything that follows.
sagan.15: Connects The photon and the full photoelectric reckoning are taken up in chapter 14 · The photon; the stopping-voltage idea and the electron-volt were laid down in Electric fields · Work and energy.
sagan.16: In 1924 a French physicist, Louis de Broglie, dared to turn the question around. If light — which we had thought a wave — can behave as a stream of particles, then perhaps matter — which we had thought particles — can behave as a wave. He proposed that every moving thing carries a wavelength of its own, given by λ = h/p on formula sheet: the greater the momentum, the shorter the wave.
sagan.17: Three years later the test was made: a beam of electrons was fired at a thin crystal. Out the other side came concentric rings cast upon a screen — the very same family of pattern that X-rays of equal wavelength weave through that same crystal, as panel (c) of Figure 1.1 reveals. The electron — that selfsame particle accelerated by voltages and steered into circles by magnetic fields in Unit 3 — diffracts.
sagan.18: A cricket ball does not diffract through a doorway, and the reason is that h is so vanishingly small. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ carries a de Broglie wavelength of about 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m — far tinier than any gap it could ever slip through, and diffraction only stirs into notice when the wavelength is near the size of the opening. Electrons are light enough that their wavelengths match the spacing between atoms themselves, which is why a crystal serves them as a diffraction grating.
sagan.19: The deepest, strangest version of the experiment is the one Simulation 1.1 lays bare. Electrons — or photons — are sent through Young's two slits one at a time. Each lands on the screen as a single sharp point of arrival: a particle, beyond doubt. Yet as the thousands of points pile up, the interference fringes rise up out of the sheer statistics of them: a wave, beyond doubt. No single particle paints a pattern, and yet not one of them ever strays from it.
sagan.20: Connects Matter waves are taken up in chapter 15 · Matter waves. The electron beam that appears there is the very one built in Motors & accelerators — the same electrons, cast in a new role.
sagan.21: Two unsolved riddles about atoms had been waiting all along, before any of these ideas arrived. First, by the classical reckoning an atom ought not to be stable at all: a classical electron sweeping around a nucleus is accelerating, and accelerating charges radiate — Maxwell's own rule, the one that lights the synchrotron — so the electron should bleed its energy away and spiral into the nucleus in the merest fraction of a second.
sagan.22: Second, glowing gases do not pour out a continuous rainbow. Each element gives off only certain sharp colours — a line spectrum, as singular as a signature. The sodium street lamp shows its two yellow lines; metal-vapour lamps and neon signs each blaze with their own line spectrum; and sunlight bears thousands of dark lines, gaps where cooler gas has stolen away precisely those colours.
sagan.23: The key is quantisation. An electron bound within an atom may hold only certain energies — discrete levels, with nothing whatever in between. A fall from a higher level to a lower one sends the atom emitting a single photon whose energy E = hf matches the gap exactly; and the swallowing of a photon of just a gap's energy lifts the electron to a higher level. The bright emission lines and the dark absorption lines map the same ladder of levels, climbed in opposite directions — so a spectrum is, in truth, the atom's energy levels written in light.
sagan.24: De Broglie's wave, too, accounts for why those levels are discrete. When the electron's wave is wrapped around the atom, it must close upon itself smoothly, seamlessly — a standing wave, like a string that will sound only its harmonics and no notes between. Only certain wavelengths fit the loop, and so only certain energies are ever allowed.
sagan.25: This picture is a simplified one, refined by the physics that came after, yet it explains the one thing the classical atom never could: why an atom has a lowest rung — a ground state — beneath which the electron cannot fall, and therefore why atoms endure at all. Quantisation is no property of light alone; it runs all the way down into the heart of matter.
sagan.26: Connects Spectra and energy levels are taken up in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; the standing waves that carve out the levels are first met alongside light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
sagan.27: One question still lingered from the wave model's first triumph. Every wave physics had ever known waves in something — so what, men asked, could carry light across the empty deep? They named the supposed medium the aether, and in 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument fine enough to catch it: two beams of light ran out along perpendicular arms, returned to interfere, and the whole apparatus was turned about and tested again through the seasons as Earth wheeled around the Sun. If Earth moves through an aether, the beam times should disagree; they never did — the most famous null result in all of physics.
sagan.28: In 1905 — the same year as the photon — Einstein accepted the result exactly as it stood and rebuilt the whole of mechanics upon two postulates: that the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and that the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, no matter how the source moves, no matter how the observer moves. The second sounds almost dull until you sit with it. For if every observer measures the very same c, then time and distance themselves must give way, and differ from one observer to the next.
sagan.29: The consequences carry names we will use all year. Moving clocks run slow — time dilation, given by t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths shrink along the line of their travel — length contraction, given by L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
sagan.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, sits only the barest hair above 1 at everyday speeds — which is precisely why the effect hid from us for three whole centuries — and then climbs without limit as speed draws near c. The little proviso "v << c" pinned to Unit 3's circular-path formula was quietly pointing to this very condition, a full unit ahead of time.
sagan.31: The proof rains down from the cosmos itself. Muons — heavy, fleeting cousins of the electron, forged kilometres overhead where cosmic rays strike the air — live on average only about 2.2 μs. Even at 0.995c that should carry them a mere 660 m or so, and yet muons reach the ground in their multitudes. In the ground's frame of reference their inner clocks run roughly ten times slow, so they last long enough to finish the fall; in the muon's own frame the atmosphere is squeezed about tenfold, so the fall itself is short enough to finish — two tellings of the tale, one and the same outcome.
sagan.32: The satellites that fix your phone's place upon the Earth carry clocks that run slow by a few microseconds every day on account of their orbital speed; left uncorrected, GPS would wander off by kilometres a day. Each time your phone finds where it stands, it is quietly applying special relativity.
sagan.33: Connects The postulates, proper time and the muon evidence are unfolded in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. The "v << c" proviso was first met on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
sagan.34: Einstein's relativity papers of 1905 closed with a brief result that would echo louder than all the rest: mass is energy, gathered and held still. The rest energy locked inside any object is given by E0 = mc² on formula sheet, and because the exchange rate is c², a single teaspoon of water holds rest energy enough to light twenty thousand homes for a year. Set the object moving, and its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, with the kinetic share given by Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet — and at everyday speeds this collapses back into the old familiar formula, one more approximation whose fine print is now plain to see.
sagan.35: The Sun runs on this very conversion. Deep in its core, hydrogen fuses into helium, and the helium weighs a touch less than the hydrogen that made it; the missing mass departs as light. The Sun turns about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiant energy every second — sunlight, then, is the Sun's own mass, set free at the universal rate.
sagan.36: And the conversion runs both ways. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, the two annihilate, and from their vanishing fly two gamma-ray photons in opposite directions — opposite, because momentum must still come out even. Hospital PET scanners see into the living body by catching exactly these pairs of photons.
sagan.37: In the particle accelerators the conversion runs in reverse: the kinetic energy of a collision is forged into wholly new particles that did not exist before the impact. The synchrotron we met earlier as a tidy circular-motion exercise is, more truly, a machine whose every last magnet is engineered around γ.
sagan.38: Connects The full energy account is unfolded in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that pour in that energy were taken up in Motors & accelerators.
sagan.39: The whole Area of Study has now passed before us once: a wave that turned out to arrive in discrete parcels, particles that turned out to interfere, atoms held together by standing waves, and a cosmic speed limit that bends time and length and unmasks mass as energy in concentrated form. What follows is this same story, told again, deeper.

Each one holds every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and checks to be sure each idea has truly settled in.

tyson.11: The wave model makes three confident predictions. Brighter light carries more energy, so the ejected electrons should leave faster. Any colour should do the job, as long as you give the metal time to soak up energy. And in dim light, there should be a lag while the energy piles up.

The maximum kinetic energy of the electrons depends only on the light’s frequency; crank up the brightness and you get more electrons, not faster ones. Below a threshold frequency, nothing comes off at all — no matter how blinding the light. And the emission is basically instant, even in the faintest glow.
tyson.13: In 1905 Einstein dropped an idea the wave model just couldn’t swallow: light hands over its energy in indivisible chunks. Each chunk — each photon — carries an energy set by E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck’s constant, equal to 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. One photon dumps all its energy into one electron, which spends the metal’s exit toll — the work function φ — and keeps the change as kinetic energy: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet. Every observation falls right out of this: frequency sets the energy of each chunk, brightness sets only how many chunks, and a chunk is delivered whole or not at all.
tyson.14: The measuring trick comes straight from Unit 3. To pin down the electrons’ energy, you apply a reverse voltage and crank it up until even the fastest electron can’t make it across — the stopping voltage — which is W = qV not on sheet run in reverse, as a brake instead of an accelerator. The electron-volt, the energy unit born when charges get accelerated through potential differences, becomes the working currency of everything ahead.
tyson.15: Connects The photon and the full photoelectric breakdown live in chapter 14 · The photon; the stopping-voltage idea and the electron-volt were set up in Electric fields · Work and energy.
tyson.16: In 1924 a French physicist, Louis de Broglie, flipped the question on its head. If light — supposedly a wave — can act like a stream of particles, then maybe matter — supposedly particles — can act like a wave. He proposed that every moving object carries a wavelength, given by λ = h/p on formula sheet: the bigger the momentum, the shorter the wave.
tyson.17: Three years later, the test came: fire a beam of electrons at a thin crystal. Out the other side it threw concentric rings on a screen — the very same family of pattern that X-rays of equal wavelength make through the same crystal, as panel (c) of Figure 1.1 shows. The electron — the particle you accelerated with voltages and steered into circular paths with magnetic fields back in Unit 3 — diffracts.
tyson.18: A cricket ball doesn’t diffract through a doorway because h is absurdly tiny. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ has a de Broglie wavelength of about 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m — way smaller than any gap it could squeeze through, and diffraction only shows up when the wavelength is comparable to the opening. Electrons are light enough that their wavelengths line up with the spacing between atoms, which is exactly why crystals work as ready-made diffraction gratings for them.
tyson.19: The most mind-bending version of the experiment is the one Simulation 1.1 puts in front of you. Electrons — or photons — go through Young’s double slits one at a time. Each one lands as a single tiny dot: a particle. But as thousands of dots stack up, the interference fringes rise out of the statistics: a wave. No single particle makes a pattern, yet every single particle obeys one. Think about that.
tyson.20: Connects Matter waves get the full workup in chapter 15 · Matter waves. The electron beam starring there is the one built in Motors & accelerators — same electrons, new gig.
tyson.21: Two nagging facts about atoms were already on the table before any of this. First, classical physics says atoms shouldn’t even be stable: a classical electron circling a nucleus is accelerating, and accelerating charges radiate — that’s Maxwell’s own rule, the one behind the synchrotron’s glow — so the electron should bleed its energy away and spiral into the nucleus in a fraction of a second. We’d be toast.
tyson.22: Second, glowing gases don’t put out a smooth rainbow. Each element emits only certain razor-sharp colours — a line spectrum, a fingerprint unique to that element. Sodium street lamps throw their two yellow lines; metal vapour lamps and neon signs each flash their own line spectrum; sunlight carries thousands of dark lines where cooler gas has stolen exactly those colours.
tyson.23: The answer is quantisation. An electron bound in an atom can hold only certain energies — discrete levels, with absolutely nothing in between. Drop from a higher level to a lower one and the atom spits out a single photon whose energy E = hf exactly matches the gap; soak up a photon of exactly a gap’s worth of energy and the electron jumps to a higher level. Bright emission lines and dark absorption lines are the same set of levels read in opposite directions — so a spectrum is basically the atom’s energy levels laid bare.
tyson.24: De Broglie’s wave even explains why those levels are discrete. Wrap the electron’s wave around the atom and it has to close on itself smoothly — a standing wave, just like a string that sounds only its harmonics. Only certain wavelengths fit the loop, so only certain energies are allowed. No in-betweens.
tyson.25: It’s a simplified picture, sharpened by later physics, but it cracks the one thing the classical atom never could: why an atom has a lowest level — a ground state — below which the electron simply can’t fall, and therefore why atoms hold together at all. And quantisation isn’t a light thing — it’s baked into matter itself.
tyson.26: Connects Spectra and energy levels get the full treatment in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; the standing waves behind the levels show up first with light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
tyson.27: One loose end dangled from the wave model’s first triumph. Every wave physics knew waves in a medium — so what was carrying light across empty space? Physicists named the proposed medium the aether, and in 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument precise enough to catch it: two light beams ran along perpendicular arms, recombined to interfere, and the whole rig was rotated and re-tested through the seasons as Earth swung around the Sun. If Earth plows through an aether, the beam times should differ. They never did — the most famous null result in all of physics.
tyson.28: In 1905 — the same year as the photon — Einstein took the result at face value and rebuilt mechanics from two postulates: the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, no matter how the source or the observer moves. That second one sounds harmless. It isn’t. If every observer measures the same c, then time and distance themselves have to flex between observers.
tyson.29: The fallout comes with names you’ll use all year. Moving clocks run slow — time dilation, given by t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths squeeze along the direction of travel — length contraction, given by L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
tyson.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, sits only a hair above 1 at everyday speeds — which is exactly why nobody caught the effect for three centuries — and then blows up without limit as speed closes in on c. That “v << c” tag hanging off Unit 3’s circular-path formula was flagging this very condition, a whole unit ahead of time.
tyson.31: The proof rains down from cosmic rays. Muons — heavy, short-lived cousins of the electron, born kilometres overhead when cosmic rays hit — have an average lifetime of about 2.2 μs. Even at 0.995c that’s only enough for roughly 660 m of travel, yet muons reach the ground in droves. In the ground frame of reference their internal clocks run about ten times slow, so they last long enough to finish the trip; in the muon’s frame of reference the atmosphere is squashed about tenfold, so the trip is short enough to finish — two stories, one matching outcome.
tyson.32: The satellites that find your phone carry clocks running slow by a few microseconds a day thanks to their orbital speed; leave it uncorrected and GPS positions would wander off by kilometres every single day. Your phone is quietly doing special relativity every time it figures out where you are.
tyson.33: Connects The postulates, proper time and the muon evidence get developed in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. The “v << c” tag first appeared on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
tyson.34: Einstein’s 1905 relativity papers ended with a short result that turned out to outshine everything else in them: mass is concentrated energy. The rest energy locked in any object is given by E0 = mc² on formula sheet, and because the conversion factor is c², a teaspoon of water holds enough rest energy to power twenty thousand homes with electricity for a year. Set the object moving and its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, with the kinetic share given by Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet — and at everyday speeds this collapses back to the familiar formula, one more approximation whose fine print now makes sense.
tyson.35: The Sun runs on exactly this trade. In its core, hydrogen fuses into helium, and the products weigh a touch less than the reactants; that missing mass leaves as light. The Sun burns about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiated energy every second — sunlight is the Sun’s own mass, cashed in at the universal rate.
tyson.36: And the trade runs both ways. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, both vanish and two gamma-ray photons fly off in opposite directions — opposite, because momentum still has to balance. Hospital PET scanners image your body by catching exactly these photon pairs.
tyson.37: In particle accelerators the trade runs in reverse: the kinetic energy of a collision becomes brand-new particles that didn’t exist before the smash. The synchrotron you met earlier as a tidy circular-motion exercise is, in truth, a machine with every magnet engineered around γ.
tyson.38: Connects The full energy account is developed in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that pump in that energy were covered in Motors & accelerators.
tyson.39: And there it is — the whole Area of Study, read once through: a wave that turned out to arrive in discrete chunks, particles that turned out to interfere, atoms held up by standing waves, and a speed limit that bends time and length and unmasks mass as concentrated energy. What comes next is the same material, dialled up in depth.
tyson.40: Pass 2 takes it apart over six chapters, numbered 6 to 11, each meant to be read the night before class hits the topic: light as a wave; diffraction and interference; the photon; matter waves; atoms and spectra; relativity and mass–energy. Each one packs every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and checks to make sure each idea actually landed.

Four pages cover the twenty-eight key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — light as a wave; light and matter as particles; light–matter similarities; relativity and mass–energy — with the subtleties, the classic traps, the assessors’ advice and exam-style questions with full solutions. cox.0: Unit 4 Area of Study 1 is the story of three confident old models — light as a wave, matter as particles, and space and time as a fixed stage — and the experiments that, one by one, brought each of them down. Read it as a single sweep, in one sitting. There’s nothing to solve here; just come and look.
cox.1: The question that runs underneath this whole page is a simple one: what is light? Newton was sure it was a stream of tiny particles, which he called corpuscles. His contemporary Huygens was just as sure it was a wave. And the argument went on for more than a century, because, maddeningly, both pictures explained the everyday evidence perfectly well — reflection, refraction, the sharp edges of shadows.
cox.2: And the stakes turn out to be enormous. Almost everything we know about the universe — the temperature of a star, what a distant galaxy is made of, the shape of a virus — reaches us as light. Get the story of light wrong, and you get the story of everything that rides on it wrong too.
cox.3: This Area of Study — its official question is How has understanding about the physical world changed? — unfolds in stages. Each one begins with a settled, confident model, brings in a single stubborn experimental result, and follows the model gently to the point where it breaks. Figure 1.1 gathers the evidence: stripes that particles simply cannot make, electrons flung out in a way no wave can explain, and particles of matter quietly drawing wave patterns. And by the end, even space and time themselves have to be rewritten.
cox.4: Connects The Fields material left us with a rather remarkable result: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field can keep regenerating each other, peel away from every wire and charge, and sail off through empty space — see Where the field idea is heading. This page takes that result and grows it, and then watches it overturned — not once, but twice.
cox.5: In 1801 Thomas Young seemed, at last, to settle the long argument. He sent light through two narrow slits onto a screen. If light really were Newton’s corpuscles, you’d expect two bright stripes, one tidily behind each slit. Instead the screen lit up with many: a long, even procession of bright and dark bands.
cox.6: Only a wave can do this. Where two ripples on a pond meet crest on crest, they swell together; crest on trough, they cancel each other out. A bright band marks a place where the waves from the two slits arrive in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ — and a dark band marks half a wavelength of mismatch, (n + ½)λ, exactly as overlapping sound and water waves behave. And the pattern is so beautifully regular that a single formula, Δx = λL/d on formula sheet, ties the spacing of the fringes to the wavelength. With little more than a ruler, Young had measured the wavelength of light.
cox.7: What, then, was actually waving? The answer came in the 1860s, out of material already in hand. James Clerk Maxwell showed that a changing electric field makes a changing magnetic field, which remakes the electric field, and that this self-sustaining pair has no choice but to travel — at a speed his equations pinned down at 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, the measured speed of light. So light can be pictured as a transverse electromagnetic wave, born wherever charges accelerate, and every electromagnetic wave — radio through to gamma — rushes along at the same speed c in a vacuum.
cox.8: And we’ve already met this production at work. Electrons whipped around the Australian Synchrotron’s storage ring are forever accelerating — circular motion is acceleration — and accelerating charges radiate. That’s where the machine’s light comes from. By 1900, with Young’s pattern and Maxwell’s fields in such happy agreement, the matter looked settled for good.
cox.9: Connects The wave model is built up fully in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the interference evidence in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference. The fields at play are the ones we mapped in Unit 3.
cox.10: The experiment that quietly reopened the whole question looks, at first, like nothing much. Shine ultraviolet light onto a clean metal surface, and electrons leap out of it — the photoelectric effect.
cox.11: Now, the wave model makes three confident predictions about this. Brighter light carries more energy, so the ejected electrons ought to come out faster. Any colour should do the job, given the metal a little time to soak up energy. And in dim light there should be a pause while the energy gathers.
cox.12: And experiment, gently but firmly, contradicts every one. The maximum kinetic energy of the electrons depends only on the light’s frequency; turn up the brightness and you eject more electrons, not faster ones. Below a certain threshold frequency, nothing comes out at all, no matter how bright the light. And the emission is, to all appearances, instant — even in the faintest glow.
cox.13: In 1905 Einstein offered an account the wave model simply could not stomach: light hands over its energy in indivisible little parcels. Each parcel — each photon — carries an energy given by E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck’s constant, equal to 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. One photon gives all its energy to one electron, which spends the metal’s exit fee — the work function φ — and keeps whatever is left as kinetic energy: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet. And every puzzling observation follows from this: frequency sets the energy of each parcel, brightness sets only how many parcels there are, and a parcel arrives whole or not at all.
cox.14: The way we measure this comes straight from Unit 3. To find the electrons’ energy, you apply a reverse voltage and wind it up until even the fastest electron can no longer make the crossing — the stopping voltage — which is just W = qV not on sheet turned around, working as a brake rather than an accelerator. And the electron-volt, that energy unit we defined when charges are accelerated through potential differences, becomes the working currency of everything that follows.
cox.15: Connects The photon and the full photoelectric analysis are handled in chapter 14 · The photon; the stopping-voltage idea and the electron-volt were set down in Electric fields · Work and energy.
cox.16: In 1924 a French physicist, Louis de Broglie, asked the question the other way around. If light — which we’d taken to be a wave — can behave like a stream of particles, then perhaps matter — which we’d taken to be particles — can behave like a wave. He proposed that every moving thing carries a wavelength of its own, given by λ = h/p on formula sheet: the more momentum, the shorter the wave.
cox.17: Three years later, someone tried it: a beam of electrons fired at a thin crystal. The beam came out drawing concentric rings on a screen — the very same family of pattern that X-rays of equal wavelength make through the same crystal, as panel (c) of Figure 1.1 shows. The electron — the very particle we accelerated with voltages and bent into circles with magnetic fields back in Unit 3 — diffracts.
cox.18: A cricket ball doesn’t diffract through a doorway, and that’s only because h is so vanishingly small. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ has a de Broglie wavelength of about 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m — far tinier than any gap it could ever slip through, and diffraction only becomes noticeable when the wavelength is comparable to the opening. Electrons are light enough that their wavelengths match the spacing between atoms, which is precisely why crystals make such fine diffraction gratings for them.
cox.19: The deepest version of the experiment is the one Simulation 1.1 lays out for you. Electrons — or photons — are sent through Young’s double slits one at a time. Each one lands on the screen as a single, pin-sharp dot: a particle. And yet, as thousands of dots pile up, the interference fringes emerge out of the statistics: a wave. No single particle makes a pattern, and still every single particle obeys one.
cox.20: Connects Matter waves are handled in chapter 15 · Matter waves. The electron beam there is the very one we built in Motors & accelerators — the same electrons, in a brand new role.
cox.21: Two unsolved puzzles about atoms were already waiting before any of these ideas arrived. First, on the classical story atoms simply shouldn’t exist: a classical electron circling a nucleus is accelerating, and accelerating charges radiate — Maxwell’s own rule, the very one that lights the synchrotron — so the electron ought to radiate its energy away and spiral into the nucleus in a fraction of a second.
cox.22: Second, glowing gases don’t pour out a smooth, continuous spectrum. Each element shines only at certain sharp colours — a line spectrum, as distinctive as a fingerprint. Sodium street lamps give us their two yellow lines; metal vapour lamps and neon signs each show their own set; and sunlight carries thousands of dark lines, where cooler gas has stolen away exactly those colours.
cox.23: The explanation is quantisation. An electron bound inside an atom is allowed only certain energies — discrete levels, with nothing at all in between. When it drops from a higher level to a lower one, the atom releases a single photon whose energy E = hf matches the gap exactly; and when it swallows a photon of precisely a gap’s worth of energy, the electron climbs to a higher level. Bright emission lines and dark absorption lines are the same ladder of levels, read in opposite directions — so a spectrum is, in effect, a map of the atom’s energy levels.
cox.24: And de Broglie’s wave tells us why those discrete levels exist at all. When you wrap the electron’s wave around the atom, it has to close on itself smoothly — a standing wave, just like a string that will only sing its own harmonics. Only certain wavelengths fit the loop, and so only certain energies are allowed.
cox.25: This is a simplified picture, tidied up by later physics, but it captures the one thing the classical atom never could: why an atom has a lowest rung — a ground state — beneath which the electron cannot fall, and therefore why atoms hold together at all. And quantisation isn’t a quirk of light alone; it runs right through matter.
cox.26: Connects Spectra and energy levels are handled in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; the standing waves that build the levels are introduced alongside light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
cox.27: One nagging question had lingered ever since the wave model’s first triumph. Every wave we knew of waves in something — so what on earth carries light across empty space? Physicists named this imagined medium the aether, and in 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument delicate enough to catch it: two light beams ran along arms at right angles, came back together to interfere, and the whole apparatus was turned and retested through the seasons as the Earth swept around the Sun. If the Earth moves through an aether, the beam times should differ; they never did — the most famous null result in all of physics.
cox.28: In 1905 — the very same year as the photon — Einstein took the result at face value and rebuilt mechanics from two postulates: the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, whatever the motion of the source or the observer. The second one sounds almost dull, and it is anything but. If every observer measures the same c, then time and distance themselves have to differ from one observer to the next.
cox.29: These consequences come with names we’ll use all year. Moving clocks run slow — time dilation, given by t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths shrink along the direction of travel — length contraction, given by L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
cox.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, sits only a whisker above 1 at everyday speeds — which is exactly why nobody noticed any of this for three centuries — and then climbs without limit as speed creeps up towards c. That little qualification “v << c” we hung on Unit 3’s circular-path formula was hinting at this very condition, a whole unit ahead of time.
cox.31: And we can actually watch it happen, in cosmic rays. Muons — heavy, short-lived cousins of the electron, made kilometres above our heads by cosmic rays — live, on average, only about 2.2 μs. Even at 0.995c that buys them barely 660 m of travel, and yet muons reach the ground in droves. In the ground frame of reference their internal clocks tick about ten times slow, so they last long enough to finish the journey; in the muon’s own frame of reference the atmosphere is squeezed about tenfold, so the journey is short enough to finish — two stories, one and the same outcome.
cox.32: The satellites that pin down your phone carry clocks that run slow by a few microseconds a day, just from their orbital speed; left uncorrected, GPS positions would wander off by kilometres every day. Your phone applies special relativity every single time it works out where you are.
cox.33: Connects The postulates, proper time and the muon evidence are developed in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. The “v << c” qualification first appeared on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
cox.34: Einstein’s 1905 relativity papers ended with a short, almost throwaway result that turned out to matter more than all the rest: mass is energy, packed tight. The rest energy locked inside any object is given by E0 = mc² on formula sheet, and because the conversion factor is c², a single teaspoon of water holds enough rest energy to power twenty thousand homes for a year. Set the object moving, and its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, with the kinetic share given by Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet — and at everyday speeds this folds neatly back into the familiar formula, one more approximation whose fine print we can now read.
cox.35: The Sun lives on this very conversion. Deep in its core, hydrogen fuses into helium, and the products weigh a touch less than the reactants; that missing mass leaves as light. The Sun turns about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiated energy every second — sunlight is the Sun’s own mass, converted at the universal rate.
cox.36: And the conversion runs both ways. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, the two annihilate, and two gamma-ray photons fly apart in opposite directions — opposite, because momentum still has to balance. Hospital PET scanners picture the body by catching exactly these photon pairs.
cox.37: In particle accelerators the conversion runs in reverse: the kinetic energy of a collision is spun into brand new particles that simply did not exist before the impact. The synchrotron we met earlier as a tidy circular-motion exercise is, more truthfully, a machine whose every magnet is built around γ.
cox.38: Connects The full energy account is developed in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that supply that energy were handled in Motors & accelerators.
cox.39: And there it is — the whole Area of Study, read through once: a wave that turned out to arrive in discrete little parcels, particles that turned out to interfere, atoms held together by standing waves, and a speed limit that bends time and length and reveals mass to be energy in concentrated form. What follows is all the same material again, only deeper.
