greene.17: Three years later came the test. Fire a beam of electrons at a thin crystal, and out the other side it traces concentric rings on a screen — the very same family of pattern that X-rays of equal wavelength make through that same crystal, as panel (c) of Figure 1.1 shows. The electron — the particle you accelerated with voltages and curved into circles with magnetic fields back in Unit 3 — diffracts.
greene.18: A cricket ball doesn't diffract through a doorway, and the reason is that h is so vanishingly small. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ has a de Broglie wavelength of about 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m — far tinier than any gap it could ever slip through, and diffraction only becomes noticeable when the wavelength is comparable to the opening. Electrons are light enough that their wavelengths land right at the spacing between atoms — which is exactly why crystals make such fine diffraction gratings for them.
greene.19: The deepest version of all of this is the one Simulation 1.1 lets you watch. Send electrons — or photons — through Young's double slits one at a time. Each one lands on the screen as a single sharp dot: a particle, plainly. Yet as thousands of dots pile up, the interference fringes emerge out of the statistics: a wave, just as plainly. No single particle makes a pattern, and yet every single particle obeys one.
greene.20: Connects Matter waves are worked through in chapter 15 · Matter waves. The electron beam that stars there is the one assembled in Motors & accelerators — the same electrons, cast in a new role.
greene.21: Two awkward facts about atoms were already hanging in the air before any of these ideas arrived. First, on the classical picture atoms simply shouldn't hold together: a classical electron looping around a nucleus is accelerating, and accelerating charges radiate — Maxwell's own rule, the very one that explains the synchrotron's glow — so the electron ought to bleed away its energy and spiral into the nucleus in a fraction of a second.
greene.22: Second, glowing gases refuse to emit a smooth, continuous spectrum. Each element pours out only certain sharp colours — a line spectrum, a fingerprint unique to that element. Sodium street lamps give their two yellow lines; metal-vapour lamps and neon signs each flash their own line spectrum; sunlight comes laced with thousands of dark lines, where cooler gas has stolen away exactly those colours.
greene.23: The key that unlocks all of this is quantisation. An electron bound inside an atom can hold only certain energies — discrete levels, with nothing in the gaps between. A jump from a higher level to a lower one makes the atom spit out a single photon whose energy E = hf matches the gap exactly; swallow a photon of precisely that gap's energy and the electron climbs to a higher level. Bright emission lines and dark absorption lines are the same ladder of levels read in opposite directions — so a spectrum is, quite literally, a map of the atom's energy levels.
greene.24: De Broglie's wave even tells you why those discrete levels exist at all. Wrap the electron's wave around the atom and it has to close on itself smoothly — a standing wave, just like a string that will sound only its harmonics. Only certain wavelengths fit neatly around the loop, so only certain energies are allowed.
greene.25: This is a simplified picture, one later physics sharpens considerably, but it captures the single thing the classical atom never could: why an atom has a lowest rung — a ground state — below which the electron cannot fall, and therefore why atoms are stable at all. And notice: quantisation isn't a quirk of light. It runs right through the heart of matter.
greene.26: Connects Spectra and energy levels are worked through in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; the standing waves that carve out those levels are introduced alongside light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
greene.27: One question lingered from the wave model's first triumph. Every wave physics knew of waves in something — so what could possibly carry light across empty space? Physicists named the proposed medium the aether, and in 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument delicate enough to catch it: two light beams ran down perpendicular arms, recombined to interfere, and the whole apparatus was rotated and re-tested across the seasons as Earth swept around the Sun. If Earth really plows through an aether, the beam times should disagree; they never did — the most famous null result in all of physics.
greene.28: In 1905 — the same year as the photon — Einstein took the result at face value and rebuilt mechanics from two postulates: the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, no matter how the source or the observer is moving. That second postulate sounds modest, but it is anything but. If everyone, always, measures the very same c, then time and distance themselves must bend to make it so.
greene.29: The consequences come with names you'll use all year. Moving clocks run slow — time dilation, given by t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths shrink along the direction of travel — length contraction, given by L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
greene.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, sits barely above 1 at everyday speeds — which is exactly why the effect hid for three centuries — and then climbs without limit as speed creeps toward c. That little caveat "v << c" stapled to Unit 3's circular-path formula was pointing at this very condition, a whole unit ahead of time.
greene.31: And the evidence rains down from the cosmos. Muons — heavy, short-lived cousins of the electron, born kilometres overhead when cosmic rays strike the atmosphere — live on average only about 2.2 μs. Even at 0.995c that buys roughly 660 m of travel, and yet muons reach the ground in droves. In the ground frame of reference their internal clocks tick about ten times slow, so they last long enough to finish the trip; in the muon's own frame of reference the atmosphere is squeezed about tenfold, so the trip is short enough to finish — two stories, one and the same outcome.
greene.32: The satellites that fix your phone's location carry clocks running slow by a few microseconds a day thanks to their orbital speed; leave that uncorrected and GPS positions would wander off by kilometres each day. Every time your phone pinpoints where you are, it is quietly applying special relativity.
greene.33: Connects The postulates, proper time and the muon evidence are developed in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. The "v << c" qualification first appeared on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
greene.34: Einstein's relativity papers of 1905 closed with a brief little result that turned out to outshine all the rest: mass is energy in concentrated form. The rest energy locked inside any object is given by E0 = mc² on formula sheet, and because the conversion rate is c², a single teaspoon of water holds enough rest energy to power twenty thousand homes for a year. Set the object moving and its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, with the kinetic share given by Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet — and at everyday speeds this collapses back into the familiar formula you already know, one more approximation whose fine print you can now read.
greene.35: The Sun runs on exactly this conversion. Deep in its core hydrogen fuses into helium, and the products come out weighing slightly less than the ingredients; that missing mass leaves as light. The Sun turns about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiated energy every second — sunlight, quite literally, is the Sun's own mass, converted at the universal rate.
greene.36: The conversion runs both ways. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, the two annihilate and two gamma-ray photons shoot off in opposite directions — opposite, because momentum still has to balance. Hospital PET scanners build their images of the body by catching exactly these photon pairs.
greene.37: In particle accelerators the conversion runs in reverse: the kinetic energy of a collision is poured into brand-new particles that simply did not exist before the impact. The synchrotron you met earlier as a tidy circular-motion exercise is, more honestly, a machine whose every magnet is engineered around γ.
greene.38: Connects The full energy account is developed in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that supply that energy were treated in Motors & accelerators.
greene.39: You've now read the whole Area of Study once, end to end: a wave that turned out to arrive in discrete packets, particles that turned out to interfere, atoms held together by standing waves, and a cosmic speed limit that warps time and length and reveals mass to be energy in disguise. What follows is the very same material, explored at greater depth.

hawking.1: One question organises this whole page: what is light? Newton said it was a stream of tiny particles, which he called corpuscles. His contemporary Huygens said it was a wave. The argument ran for more than a century. Both models explained the everyday evidence — reflection, refraction, shadows — equally well.
hawking.2: The stakes are high. Almost everything we know about the universe arrives as light: the temperature of a star, the composition of a galaxy, the structure of a virus. Get light wrong, and you get all of it wrong.
hawking.3: The official question of this Area of Study is How has understanding about the physical world changed? It proceeds in stages. By the end, space and time are revised too.
hawking.4: Connects The Fields material ended on a striking result. This page builds on that result. Then it watches that result overturned, twice.

A bright band marks where the waves from the two slits arrive in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ. A dark band marks a half-wavelength of mismatch, (n + ½)λ, exactly as overlapping sound and water waves behave. The pattern is so regular that one formula, Δx = λL/d on formula sheet, ties the fringe spacing to the wavelength. Young had measured the wavelength of light with little more than a ruler.
hawking.7: What was waving? The answer came in the 1860s, from physics already in hand. James Clerk Maxwell showed that a changing electric field creates a changing magnetic field, which recreates the electric field, and that this self-sustaining pair must travel — at a speed his equations fixed at 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, the measured speed of light. hawking.8: We have already met this kind of production. nye.25: Now this is a simplified model, tidied up by later physics, but it nails the one thing the classical atom couldn’t: why an atom has a lowest level — a ground state — below which the electron simply can’t fall, and therefore why atoms hold together at all. And quantisation isn’t just a light thing — it’s baked right into matter!
nye.26: Connects Spectra and energy levels get the full treatment in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; the standing waves that build those levels are introduced right alongside light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
nye.27: Now here’s one loose end left dangling from the wave model’s first big win. Every wave known to physics waves in a medium — so what on Earth carries light across empty space? Physicists named the supposed medium the aether, and in 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument precise enough to catch it: two light beams ran along perpendicular arms, recombined to interfere, and the whole rig got spun around and re-tested through the seasons as Earth swung around the Sun. If Earth plows through an aether, the beam times should differ — but they never did! It’s the most famous null result in all of physics.
nye.28: Then in 1905 — the very same year as the photon — Einstein took the result exactly as it stood and rebuilt mechanics from two postulates: the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, no matter how the source or the observer is moving. That second postulate sounds totally harmless — but it isn’t! If every observer measures the same c, then time and distance themselves have to differ between observers.
nye.29: And the consequences have names you’ll use all year! Moving clocks run slow — that’s time dilation, given by t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths shrink along the direction of travel — that’s length contraction, given by L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
nye.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, sits just a hair above 1 at everyday speeds — which is exactly why nobody spotted the effect for three centuries — and then it shoots up without limit as speed creeps toward c. That qualification “v << c” tacked onto Unit 3’s circular-path formula was flagging this very condition, a whole unit in advance!
nye.31: And we actually see this in cosmic rays! Muons — heavy, short-lived cousins of the electron, made kilometres overhead by cosmic rays — have an average lifetime of about 2.2 μs. Even at 0.995c that’s only enough for roughly 660 m of travel, yet muons hit the ground in huge numbers! In the ground frame of reference their internal clocks run about ten times slow, so they last long enough to finish the trip; in the muon’s frame of reference the atmosphere is squashed about tenfold, so the trip is short enough to finish — two descriptions, one consistent outcome.
nye.32: The satellites that pinpoint your phone carry clocks that run slow by a few microseconds each day thanks to their orbital speed; left uncorrected, GPS positions would drift by kilometres a day! So your phone is quietly running special relativity every single time it figures out where you are.
nye.33: Connects Postulates, proper time and the muon evidence get developed in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. That “v << c” qualification first showed up on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
nye.34: Einstein’s 1905 relativity papers wrapped up with a short little result that turned out to matter more than all the rest: mass is just a concentrated form of energy! The rest energy locked up in any object is given by E0 = mc² on formula sheet, and because the conversion factor is c², a single teaspoon of water holds enough rest energy to power twenty thousand homes with electricity for a year! Set the object moving and its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, with the kinetic share given by Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet — and at everyday speeds this collapses right back into the familiar formula, one more approximation whose fine print is now crystal clear.
nye.35: And the Sun runs on exactly this conversion! Down in its core, hydrogen fuses into helium, and the products weigh slightly less than the reactants; that missing mass shoots out as light. The Sun turns about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiated energy every single second — sunlight literally is the Sun’s mass, converted at the universal rate.
nye.36: And it runs both ways! When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, both vanish and two gamma-ray photons fly apart in opposite directions — opposite, because momentum still has to balance. Hospital PET scanners image your body by catching exactly these photon pairs!
nye.37: In particle accelerators the conversion runs in reverse: the kinetic energy of a collision turns into brand-new particles that didn’t even exist before the impact! That synchrotron we studied earlier as a tidy circular-motion exercise is, more precisely, a machine whose every single magnet is engineered around γ.
nye.38: Connects The full energy account gets developed in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that pour in that energy were covered in Motors & accelerators.
nye.39: And there you have it — you’ve now read the whole Area of Study once through: a wave that turned out to arrive in discrete chunks, particles that turned out to interfere, atoms explained by standing waves, and a speed limit that warps time and length and reveals mass as concentrated energy! What comes next is this exact same material, dialled up to greater depth.

munroe.0: Unit 4 Area of Study 1 is about three ideas everyone was sure of — light is a wave, matter is made of particles, and space and time just sit there as a fixed background — and the experiments that knocked down all three. Read it as one overview. There’s nothing to solve here; just things to find out.
munroe.1: The question that runs through this whole page is a simple one: what is light? Newton thought it was a stream of tiny particles and called them corpuscles. His contemporary Huygens thought it was a wave. They argued about it for over a century, and the reason it dragged on is that both models explained the everyday stuff just fine — reflection, refraction, shadows. Neither side could land a knockout.
munroe.2: This matters more than it sounds. Almost everything we know about the universe — how hot a star is, what a galaxy is made of, the shape of a virus — shows up as light. If your account of light is wrong, then your account of everything you learned from light is wrong too.
munroe.3: This Area of Study — the official question is How has understanding about the physical world changed? — goes in stages. Each stage starts with a model everyone trusted, drops one stubborn experimental result on it, and follows the model until it breaks. Figure 1.1 lays out the evidence: stripes that particles simply cannot make, electrons getting kicked out in a way waves cannot explain, and particles of matter making wave patterns. By the end, even space and time get rewritten.
munroe.4: Connects The Fields material ended on a big result: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field can keep regenerating each other, peel away from every wire and charge that made them, and travel through completely empty space — see Where the field idea is heading. This page takes that result and runs with it, and then watches it get overturned. Twice.
munroe.5: In 1801 Thomas Young looked like he’d ended the argument. He sent light through two narrow slits onto a screen. If light were Newton’s corpuscles, you’d expect two bright stripes, one behind each slit. That’s not what showed up. The screen had lots of stripes — a long run of bright and dark bands, evenly spaced.
munroe.6: Only a wave does that. Where two ripples on a pond meet crest on crest, they add up; crest on trough, they cancel out. A bright band sits where the waves from the two slits show up in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ — and a dark band sits where they’re half a wavelength out, (n + ½)λ, exactly like overlapping sound and water waves do. The pattern is so tidy that a single formula, Δx = λL/d on formula sheet, ties the fringe spacing to the wavelength. So Young had measured the wavelength of light with little more than a ruler.
munroe.7: What was actually waving got answered in the 1860s, out of physics that was already on the books. James Clerk Maxwell showed that a changing electric field makes a changing magnetic field, which remakes the electric field, and that this self-feeding pair has to move — at a speed his equations pinned at 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, which is the measured speed of light. So light can be modelled as a transverse electromagnetic wave, made any time charges accelerate, and every electromagnetic wave — radio all the way up to gamma — goes at the same speed c in a vacuum.
munroe.8: You’ve already met a real example of this. The electrons forced around the Australian Synchrotron’s storage ring are accelerating the whole time — going in a circle is accelerating — and accelerating charges radiate. That’s why the machine gives off light. By 1900, with Young’s pattern and Maxwell’s fields telling the same story, the question looked closed.
munroe.9: Connects The wave model gets the full treatment in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the interference evidence in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference. The fields doing the work here are the ones mapped out in Unit 3.
munroe.10: The experiment that pried the question back open looks like nothing special. Shine ultraviolet light on a clean metal surface and electrons get knocked off it. That’s the photoelectric effect.
munroe.11: The wave model makes three predictions about this. Brighter light carries more energy, so the electrons that come off should leave faster. Any colour should work, as long as you give the metal time to soak up enough energy. And in dim light there should be a wait while the energy piles up.
munroe.12: Every one of those predictions was wrong. The maximum kinetic energy of the electrons depends only on the light’s frequency; turning up the brightness gives you more electrons, not faster ones. Below a threshold frequency you get nothing at all, no matter how bright the light. And the electrons come off basically instantly, even in the dimmest light you can manage.
munroe.13: In 1905 Einstein gave an explanation the wave model just couldn’t hold: light hands over its energy in lumps that can’t be split. Each lump — each photon — carries an energy of E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck’s constant, equal to 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. One photon dumps all its energy into one electron, which spends the metal’s exit fee — the work function φ — and keeps whatever’s left over as kinetic energy: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet. Every observation drops out of this: frequency sets the energy of each lump, brightness sets only how many lumps there are, and a lump arrives all-or-nothing.
munroe.14: The way you measure this comes straight from Unit 3. To find out the electrons’ energy, you put a reverse voltage on them and crank it up until even the fastest electron can’t make it across — that’s the stopping voltage — which is just W = qV not on sheet run backwards, as a brake instead of an accelerator. The electron-volt, the energy unit you defined when you pushed charges through potential differences, becomes the everyday unit for everything that comes next.
munroe.15: Connects The photon and the full photoelectric analysis are in chapter 14 · The photon; the stopping-voltage idea and the electron-volt got set up back in Electric fields · Work and energy.
munroe.16: In 1924 a French physicist, Louis de Broglie, asked the question the other way round. If light — which everyone calls a wave — can act like a stream of particles, then maybe matter — which everyone calls particles — can act like a wave. He said every moving object carries a wavelength, given by λ = h/p on formula sheet: more momentum, shorter wave.
munroe.17: Three years later somebody actually tried it: they fired a beam of electrons at a thin crystal. The beam came out the far side making concentric rings on a screen — the very same kind of pattern X-rays of the same wavelength make going through the same crystal, which is what panel (c) of Figure 1.1 shows. The electron, the particle you accelerated with voltages and bent into circles with magnetic fields in Unit 3, diffracts.
munroe.18: A cricket ball doesn’t diffract through a doorway because h is so tiny. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ has a de Broglie wavelength of roughly 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m — way smaller than any gap it could ever fit through, and diffraction only becomes noticeable when the wavelength is about the size of the opening. Electrons are light enough that their wavelengths land near the spacing between atoms, which is exactly why crystals work as diffraction gratings for them.
munroe.19: The cleanest version of all this is the one Simulation 1.1 shows you. Send electrons — or photons — through Young’s double slits one at a time. Each one lands on the screen as a single tiny dot: a particle. But let thousands of dots pile up and the interference fringes build themselves out of the statistics: a wave. No single particle makes a pattern, and yet every single particle obeys one.
munroe.20: Connects Matter waves are covered in chapter 15 · Matter waves. The electron beam there is the one you built in Motors & accelerators — same electrons, new job.
munroe.21: Two awkward facts about atoms came before any of these ideas. First, by the classical rules atoms shouldn’t even be able to exist: a classical electron circling a nucleus is accelerating, and accelerating charges radiate — that’s Maxwell’s own rule, the one that explains the synchrotron’s glow — so the electron should radiate away all its energy and spiral into the nucleus in a fraction of a second. It doesn’t.
munroe.22: Second, glowing gases don’t give off a smooth, continuous spectrum. Each element gives off only certain sharp colours — a line spectrum, and it’s a fingerprint for that element. Sodium street lamps put out their two yellow lines; metal vapour lamps and neon signs each show their own set of lines; sunlight carries thousands of dark lines where cooler gas has pulled out exactly those colours.
munroe.23: The explanation is quantisation. An electron stuck in an atom can only hold certain energies — discrete levels, with nothing allowed in between. Drop from a higher level to a lower one and the atom spits out a single photon whose energy E = hf matches the gap exactly; absorb a photon of exactly a gap’s worth of energy and the electron jumps up a level. Bright emission lines and dark absorption lines are the same set of levels read in opposite directions, so a spectrum is basically a readout of the atom’s energy levels.
munroe.24: De Broglie’s wave even explains why the levels are discrete in the first place. Wrap the electron’s wave around the atom and it has to join up with itself smoothly — a standing wave, like a string that only sounds its harmonics. Only certain wavelengths fit the loop, so only certain energies are allowed.
munroe.25: This is a simplified picture, tidied up by later physics, but it nails the one thing the classical atom couldn’t: why an atom has a lowest level — a ground state — that the electron can’t fall below, and so why atoms are stable at all. And quantisation isn’t just a light thing; it’s built into matter.
munroe.26: Connects Spectra and energy levels are covered in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; the standing waves that make the levels first show up alongside light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
munroe.27: One question hung over the wave model from its first big win. Every wave physics knew about waves in something — so what carries light across empty space? Physicists named the proposed medium the aether, and in 1887 Michelson and Morley built a machine sensitive enough to catch it: two light beams ran along arms at right angles, recombined to interfere, and the whole rig was spun around and re-tested through the seasons as Earth went around the Sun. If Earth is moving through an aether, the beam times should come out different; they never did — the most famous nothing-happened in physics.
munroe.28: In 1905 — the same year as the photon — Einstein took the result at face value and rebuilt mechanics from two postulates: the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, no matter how the source or the observer is moving. The second one sounds harmless and is not. If everyone measures the same c, then time and distance themselves have to come out different for different observers.
munroe.29: The consequences have names you’ll use all year. Moving clocks run slow — time dilation, given by t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths squash up along the direction they’re travelling — length contraction, given by L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
munroe.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, is only a hair above 1 at everyday speeds — which is exactly why nobody noticed any of this for three centuries — and it blows up without limit as speed closes in on c. The little "v << c" tag stuck on Unit 3’s circular-path formula was flagging this condition a whole unit early.
munroe.31: The proof comes down from the sky, in cosmic rays. Muons — heavy, short-lived cousins of the electron, made kilometres overhead when cosmic rays hit the atmosphere — live about 2.2 μs on average. Even at 0.995c that’s only enough for about 660 m of travel, yet muons reach the ground in big numbers. In the ground frame of reference their internal clocks tick about ten times slow, so they last long enough to finish the trip; in the muon’s frame of reference the atmosphere is squashed to about a tenth, so the trip is short enough to finish — two descriptions, one answer.
munroe.32: The satellites that find your phone carry clocks that run slow by a few microseconds a day because of how fast they’re moving; leave that uncorrected and GPS positions would drift kilometres off every day. Your phone is doing special relativity every single time it figures out where you are.
munroe.33: Connects The postulates, proper time and the muon evidence are developed in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. The "v << c" tag was first slapped on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
munroe.34: Einstein’s 1905 relativity papers ended with a short result that turned out to matter more than all the rest of it: mass is just energy, packed up tight. The rest energy in any object is E0 = mc² on formula sheet, and because the conversion factor is c², a teaspoon of water holds enough rest energy to run twenty thousand homes for a year. Once the object is moving, its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, and the kinetic part of that is Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet — at everyday speeds this collapses back to the familiar formula, which is one more approximation whose fine print you can now read.
munroe.35: The Sun runs on exactly this conversion. Down in its core, hydrogen fuses into helium, and the helium weighs a tiny bit less than the hydrogen that went in; the missing mass leaves as light. The Sun turns about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiated energy every second — sunlight is the Sun’s own mass, cashed in at the universal rate.
munroe.36: This conversion runs both ways. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, both vanish and two gamma-ray photons fly off in opposite directions — opposite, because momentum still has to balance. Hospital PET scanners image your body by catching exactly these photon pairs.
munroe.37: In particle accelerators the conversion goes the other direction: the kinetic energy of a collision turns into brand-new particles that didn’t exist before the smash. bronowski.0: Unit 4 Area of Study 1 turns on three pictures of the world that men once held to be settled — light as a wave, matter as particles, and space and time as a fixed stage — and on the experiments that, one by one, brought each of them down. Read it as a single sweep. Nothing here asks to be solved.
bronowski.1: The question that gathers this whole page into one is the oldest question about light: what is it? Newton was sure it was a hail of tiny particles, which he called corpuscles. Huygens, his contemporary, was just as sure it was a wave. The quarrel ran on for more than a century, and it ran on because both pictures explained the plain evidence of every day — reflection, refraction, the casting of shadows.
bronowski.2: What hangs on the answer is no small thing. Almost everything we know of the universe — the temperature of a star, what a far galaxy is made of, the shape of a virus — reaches us as light. Get light wrong, and you get wrong everything that rides upon it.
bronowski.3: This Area of Study — its formal question is How has understanding about the physical world changed? — advances in stages. By the end, even space and time are made to give ground.

This page takes that result up, carries it forward, and then watches it overturned — not once, but twice.

Were light made of Newton’s corpuscles, two bright stripes ought to stand there, one behind each slit. Instead the screen showed many — a long, even procession of bright and dark bands.
bronowski.6: Only a wave can make this happen. Where two ripples on a pond meet crest on crest, they swell together; crest on trough, they cancel and the water lies still. A bright band marks a place where the waves from the two slits arrive in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ — and a dark band marks a half-wavelength out of step, (n + ½)λ, exactly as sound and water waves behave when they overlap. Light, then, can be pictured as a transverse electromagnetic wave, born wherever charges are made to accelerate; and every electromagnetic wave, from radio to gamma, runs at the one speed c through a vacuum.
bronowski.8: An instance of this making of light has already crossed your path. The electrons driven round the storage ring of the Australian Synchrotron are forever accelerating — for circular motion is acceleration — and accelerating charges radiate. That is why the machine pours out light. By 1900, with Young’s pattern and Maxwell’s fields singing the same tune, the question seemed closed.
bronowski.9: Connects The wave model is worked out in full in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the evidence of interference in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference. The fields at work in it are those mapped in Unit 3.
bronowski.10: The experiment that prised the question open again looks, at first glance, like nothing at all. Shine ultraviolet light onto a clean metal surface, and electrons leap out of it — the photoelectric effect.
bronowski.11: The wave model makes three confident predictions about it. Brighter light carries more energy, so the electrons should come away faster. Any colour ought to do the work, given time for the metal to soak up energy. And in dim light there should be a pause while the energy gathers itself.
bronowski.12: The experiment answered no to every one. The greatest kinetic energy of the electrons hangs on the light’s frequency alone; turn up the brightness and you get more electrons, not faster ones. bronowski.13: In 1905 Einstein offered an account the wave model could not hold: light hands over its energy in whole, indivisible parcels. Each parcel — each photon — carries an energy E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck’s constant, equal to 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. If light — which we had taken for a wave — can behave as a stream of particles, then matter — which we had taken for particles — might behave as a wave. bronowski.28: In 1905 — the same year as the photon — Einstein took the result exactly as it stood and rebuilt mechanics from two postulates: that the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and that the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, whatever the motion of the source or of the watcher. The second postulate looks unremarkable, and it is anything but. If every observer measures the one same c, then time and distance themselves must come out differently for different observers.
bronowski.29: The consequences carry names you will hear all year. Moving clocks run slow — time dilation, given by t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths shrink along the line of travel — length contraction, given by L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
bronowski.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, sits only a hair above 1 at the speeds of everyday life — which is why the effect escaped notice for three centuries — and climbs without bound as speed presses toward c. That little qualification “v << c” hung on Unit 3’s circular-path formula was pointing, a whole unit ahead, to just this condition.
bronowski.31: The proof is read off the cosmic rays. Muons — heavy, fleeting cousins of the electron, struck into being kilometres overhead by cosmic rays — live on average about 2.2 μs. Even at 0.995c that allows them perhaps 660 m of travel, and yet muons arrive at the ground in great numbers. In the ground’s frame of reference their inner clocks run about ten times slow, so they last long enough to finish the journey; in the muon’s own frame of reference the atmosphere is squeezed to about a tenth, so the journey is short enough to finish — two tellings, one and the same outcome.
bronowski.32: The satellites that pinpoint your phone carry clocks running slow by a few microseconds a day on account of their orbital speed; left uncorrected, the positions they give would drift off by kilometres each day. Every time your phone finds where it is, it is quietly applying special relativity.
bronowski.33: Connects The postulates, proper time and the muon evidence are developed in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. The “v << c” qualification was first met on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
bronowski.34: Einstein’s relativity papers of 1905 ended on a brief result that came to matter more than all the rest: that mass is energy gathered up in a dense form. The rest energy held in any object is E0 = mc² on formula sheet, and because the rate of exchange is c², a teaspoon of water holds rest energy enough to light twenty thousand homes for a year. Set the object moving, and its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, of which the kinetic share is Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet — and at everyday speeds this folds back into the familiar old formula, one more approximation whose small print is now in plain sight.
bronowski.35: The Sun lives by this exchange. In its core, hydrogen fuses into helium, and what comes out weighs slightly less than what went in; the missing mass departs as light. The Sun turns about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiated energy every second — sunlight is the Sun’s own mass, traded away at the universal rate.
bronowski.36: The exchange runs both ways. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, both are annihilated, and two gamma-ray photons fly apart in opposite directions — opposite, because momentum must still come out even. Hospital PET scanners read the body by catching exactly these pairs of photons.
bronowski.37: In a particle accelerator the exchange is driven in reverse: the kinetic energy of a collision is forged into new particles that had no existence before the impact. The synchrotron we met earlier as an exercise in circular motion is, more truly, a machine whose every magnet is built around γ.
bronowski.38: Connects The full account of energy is developed in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that furnish that energy were taken up in Motors & accelerators.
bronowski.39: The whole Area of Study has now been walked through once: a wave that turned out to arrive in discrete parcels, particles that turned out to interfere, atoms held together by standing waves, and a speed limit that bends time and length and reveals mass to be energy gathered up tight. What follows is the same ground, gone over more deeply.
bronowski.40: Pass 2 unfolds it across six chapters, numbered 6 to 11, each meant to be read the night before the class meets the topic: light as a wave; diffraction and interference; the photon; matter waves; atoms and spectra; relativity and mass–energy. Each holds every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and checks to make sure each idea has truly taken hold.
bronowski.41: Pass 3 is for the work of preparing for the exam. Four pages cover the twenty-eight key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — light as a wave; light and matter as particles; light–matter similarities; relativity and mass–energy — together with the subtleties, the common errors, the assessors’ advice and exam-style questions with full solutions. einstein.0: Unit 4 Area of Study 1 takes up three models I once thought settled — light as a wave, matter as particles, and space and time as fixed — and the experiments that quietly toppled each of them. I should like you to read this through as one continuous picture. Nothing here asks you to solve anything.
einstein.1: The question that holds this whole page together is the simplest I know: what is light? Newton was sure it was a stream of tiny particles, which he called corpuscles. His contemporary Huygens was equally sure it was a wave. The quarrel ran on for more than a century, for both pictures accounted handsomely for the everyday evidence of reflection, refraction and shadows.
einstein.2: What follows from this is no small thing: almost everything we know of the universe — the temperature of a star, the makeup of a galaxy, the shape of a virus — reaches us as light. Get light wrong, and you have got wrong everything that rides upon it.
einstein.3: This Area of Study — whose official question is How has understanding about the physical world changed? — I shall unfold in stages. Each stage opens with a model thought secure, sets beside it one stubborn experimental result, and follows the model until it breaks. Figure 1.1 gathers the evidence: stripes that particles can never make, electrons flung out in a way no wave can explain, and particles of matter that trace out wave patterns. By the end, even space and time themselves are remade.
einstein.4: Connects The Fields material closed with a result I find quite beautiful: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field can give birth to one another, tear themselves free of every wire and charge, and travel on through empty space — see Where the field idea is heading. This page carries that result forward, and then shows it overturned, not once but twice.
einstein.5: In 1801 Thomas Young seemed to settle the old quarrel for good. He passed light through two narrow slits onto a screen. Were light made of Newton’s corpuscles, you should see two bright stripes, one behind each slit. Instead the screen showed a great many: a long parade of evenly spaced bright and dark bands.
einstein.6: Only a wave can do this. Where two ripples on a pond meet crest upon crest, they swell together; crest upon trough, they cancel. A bright band marks a place where the waves from the two slits arrive in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ — and a dark band marks half a wavelength out of step, (n + ½)λ, exactly as overlapping sound and water waves behave. The pattern is so orderly that a single formula, Δx = λL/d on formula sheet, ties the fringe spacing to the wavelength. With little more than a ruler, Young had measured the wavelength of light.
einstein.7: What it was that waved came to be answered in the 1860s, from material already in hand. James Clerk Maxwell showed that a changing electric field makes a changing magnetic field, which makes the electric field again, and that this self-sustaining pair must travel — at a speed his equations pinned at 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, the measured speed of light. Light may therefore be pictured as a transverse electromagnetic wave, born wherever charges accelerate, and every electromagnetic wave — from radio to gamma — travels at the same speed c in a vacuum.
einstein.8: You have already met one such birth of light. Electrons driven round the Australian Synchrotron’s storage ring are forever accelerating — for circular motion is acceleration — and accelerating charges radiate. That is why the machine pours out light. By 1900, with Young’s pattern and Maxwell’s fields singing the same tune, the question looked closed.
einstein.9: Connects The wave model is set out in full in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the interference evidence in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference. The fields at work are those charted in Unit 3.
einstein.10: The experiment that pried the question open again looks almost dull. Shine ultraviolet light onto a clean metal surface, and electrons leap out of it — the photoelectric effect.
einstein.11: The wave model makes three confident predictions about it. Brighter light carries more energy, so the ejected electrons ought to leave faster. Any colour ought to work, given the metal time enough to soak up energy. And in dim light there ought to be a pause while the energy gathers.
einstein.12: Experiment contradicted every one. The greatest kinetic energy of the electrons depends only on the light’s frequency; turning up the brightness throws out more electrons, not faster ones. Below a threshold frequency, not a single electron escapes, however bright the light. And the emission is all but instantaneous, even in the faintest glimmer.
einstein.13: In 1905 I offered an account the wave model simply could not hold: light hands over its energy in indivisible parcels. Each parcel — each photon — carries an energy E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck’s constant, equal to 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. One photon gives all its energy to one electron, which spends the metal’s exit cost — the work function φ — and keeps the rest as kinetic energy: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet. Every observation falls out of this: frequency fixes the energy of each parcel, brightness fixes only how many parcels arrive, and a parcel is handed over whole or not at all.
einstein.14: The way of measuring this comes straight from Unit 3. To learn the electrons’ energy, you apply a reverse voltage and raise it until even the swiftest electron can no longer cross — the stopping voltage — which is W = qV not on sheet turned about, working as a brake rather than an accelerator. The electron-volt, the energy unit we defined when charges are accelerated through potential differences, becomes the working coin of all that follows.
einstein.15: Connects The photon and the full photoelectric analysis are treated in chapter 14 · The photon; the stopping-voltage idea and the electron-volt were established in Electric fields · Work and energy.
einstein.16: In 1924 a French physicist, Louis de Broglie, turned the question on its head. If light, which we take to be a wave, plays the part of a stream of particles, then matter, which we take to be particles, might play the part of a wave. He proposed that every moving thing carries a wavelength, given by λ = h/p on formula sheet: the greater the momentum, the shorter the wave.
einstein.17: Three years on the test was made: a beam of electrons was fired at a thin crystal. Out of it came concentric rings on a screen — the very same family of pattern that X-rays of equal wavelength make through the same crystal, as panel (c) of Figure 1.1 shows. The electron — that same particle we accelerated with voltages and steered round circles with magnetic fields in Unit 3 — diffracts.
einstein.18: A cricket ball does not diffract through a doorway, and only because h is so vanishingly small. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ has a de Broglie wavelength of about 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m — far tinier than any gap it might slip through, and diffraction shows itself only when the wavelength is near the size of the opening. Electrons are light enough that their wavelengths match the spacing between atoms, which is just why crystals serve them as ready-made diffraction gratings.
einstein.19: The deepest version of the experiment is the one Simulation 1.1 lays bare. Electrons — or photons — are sent through Young’s double slits one at a time. Each lands on the screen as a single small dot: a particle. Yet as thousands of dots pile up, the interference fringes emerge out of the statistics: a wave. No single particle makes a pattern, and still every single particle obeys one.
einstein.20: Connects Matter waves are treated in chapter 15 · Matter waves. The electron beam featured there is the one built in Motors & accelerators — the same electrons in a new role.
einstein.21: Two unsettled facts about atoms came before all these ideas. First, on the old classical reckoning atoms ought not to be stable at all: a classical electron circling a nucleus is accelerating, and accelerating charges radiate — Maxwell’s own rule, the very one that explains the synchrotron’s glow — so the electron should radiate its energy away and spiral into the nucleus in a fraction of a second.
einstein.22: Second, glowing gases do not pour out a continuous spectrum. Each element gives off only certain sharp colours — a line spectrum, peculiar to itself. Sodium street lamps show their two yellow lines; metal vapour lamps and neon signs each wear their own line spectrum; sunlight carries thousands of dark lines where cooler gas has taken away exactly those colours.
einstein.23: The explanation is quantisation. An electron bound in an atom may hold only certain energies — discrete levels, with nothing in between. A jump from a higher level to a lower one makes the atom give off a single photon whose energy E = hf matches the gap exactly; and swallowing a photon of just a gap’s energy lifts the electron to a higher level. Bright emission lines and dark absorption lines answer to the same set of levels, read in opposite directions, so a spectrum lays bare the atom’s energy levels.
einstein.24: De Broglie’s wave accounts for the discrete levels too. When the electron’s wave is wrapped round the atom, it must close on itself smoothly — a standing wave, like a string that will sound only its harmonics. Only certain wavelengths fit the loop, and so only certain energies are allowed.
einstein.25: The description is a simplified picture, refined by the physics that came after, but it gives the one feature the classical atom could never give: why an atom has a lowest level — a ground state — beneath which the electron cannot fall, and so why atoms are stable at all. Quantisation is no special property of light; it lies at the very root of matter.
einstein.26: Connects Spectra and energy levels are treated in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; the standing waves that produce the levels are introduced with light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
einstein.27: One question lingered from the wave model’s first triumph. Every wave known to physics waves in some medium — so what, men asked, carries light across empty space? Physicists named the supposed medium the aether, and in 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument fine enough to catch it: two light beams ran along arms set at right angles, joined again to interfere, and the whole apparatus was turned and tested afresh through the seasons as the Earth swung round the Sun. If the Earth moves through an aether, the beam times should differ; they never did — the most celebrated null result in all of physics.
einstein.28: In 1905 — the same year as the photon — I took the result just as it stood and rebuilt mechanics upon two postulates: that the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and that the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, whatever the motion of the source or the watcher. The second looks innocent, yet it is not. If every observer measures the same c, then time and distance themselves must differ from one observer to the next.
einstein.29: The consequences bear names we shall use all year. Moving clocks run slow — time dilation, given by t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths shrink along the direction of travel — length contraction, given by L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
einstein.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, sits only the barest fraction above 1 at everyday speeds — which is why the effect lay hidden for three centuries — and climbs without limit as the speed nears c. The little caveat "v << c" pinned to Unit 3’s circular-path formula was pointing at just this condition, a whole unit before its time.
einstein.31: The evidence comes to us from cosmic rays. Muons — heavy, short-lived cousins of the electron, born kilometres overhead where cosmic rays strike — live on average about 2.2 μs. Even at 0.995c that allows only some 660 m of travel, yet muons reach the ground in great numbers. In the ground frame of reference their inner clocks run about ten times slow, so they last long enough to finish the trip; in the muon’s own frame of reference the atmosphere is squeezed about tenfold, so the trip is short enough to finish — two stories, one and the same outcome.
einstein.32: The satellites that pin down your phone carry clocks running slow by a few microseconds each day on account of their orbital speed; left uncorrected, GPS positions would wander by kilometres a day. Your phone applies special relativity every time it works out where you are.
einstein.33: Connects Postulates, proper time and the muon evidence are developed in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. The "v << c" qualification was introduced on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
einstein.34: My relativity papers of 1905 ended with a brief result that proved weightier than all the rest: mass is a concentrated form of energy. The rest energy held in any object is given by E0 = mc² on formula sheet, and because the conversion factor is c², a teaspoon of water holds enough rest energy to light twenty thousand homes for a year. Set the object moving and its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, with the kinetic share given by Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet — and at everyday speeds this folds back into the familiar formula, one more approximation whose small caveat is now plain to see.
einstein.35: The Sun runs on this very conversion. In its core, hydrogen fuses into helium, and the products weigh a little less than the reactants; the missing mass goes out as light. The Sun turns about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiated energy each second — sunlight is the Sun’s own mass, converted at the universal rate.
einstein.36: The conversion runs both ways. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, both are annihilated and two gamma-ray photons fly apart in opposite directions — opposite, for momentum must still balance. Hospital PET scanners picture the body by catching exactly these photon pairs.
einstein.37: In particle accelerators the conversion runs in reverse: the kinetic energy of a collision is turned into new particles that did not exist before the impact. The synchrotron we studied earlier as a tidy exercise in circular motion is, more truly, a machine whose every magnet is engineered around γ.
einstein.38: Connects The full energy account is developed in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that supply that energy were treated in Motors & accelerators.
einstein.39: The whole Area of Study has now been read through once: a wave that turned out to arrive in discrete parcels, particles that turned out to interfere, atoms explained by standing waves, and a speed limit that bends time and length and reveals mass as concentrated energy. What follows is the same material, gone into more deeply.

veritasium.0: For three centuries, three ideas looked completely safe: light is a wave, matter is made of particles, and space and time just sit there as a fixed backdrop. Unit 4 Area of Study 1 is the story of how experiments demolished every one of them. Read it straight through as a single overview. Nothing here asks you to solve anything.
veritasium.1: The question that runs underneath this whole page sounds simple: what is light? Newton was certain it was a stream of tiny particles — he called them corpuscles. His rival Huygens was just as certain it was a wave. You might expect that to get settled quickly, but the argument dragged on for more than a century, because both models explained everything people could see: reflection, refraction, shadows. The everyday evidence couldn't tell them apart.
veritasium.2: And the stakes are enormous. Almost everything we know about the universe — the temperature of a star, what a distant galaxy is made of, the shape of a virus — reaches us as light. So if you get light wrong, you don't just get one thing wrong. You get everything that rides on it wrong.
veritasium.3: This Area of Study — its official question is How has understanding about the physical world changed? — goes stage by stage. Each stage starts with a model everyone trusted, hands you one stubborn experimental result, and then watches the model break under it. Figure 1.1 lays out the evidence: stripes that particles simply cannot make, electrons flying off in a way no wave can explain, and lumps of matter producing wave patterns. By the end, even space and time get rewritten.
veritasium.4: Connects The Fields material ended on a striking result: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field can keep regenerating each other, break free of every wire and charge, and sail off through empty space — see Where the field idea is heading. This page takes that result and runs with it — and then watches it get overturned, not once but twice.
veritasium.5: In 1801 Thomas Young looked like he'd ended the argument for good. He sent light through two narrow slits onto a screen. If light were really Newton's corpuscles, you'd expect two bright stripes, one behind each slit — nothing more. But that's not what showed up. The screen lit up with many bands: a long row of evenly spaced bright and dark stripes.
veritasium.6: And only a wave can do that. Picture two ripples meeting on a pond: crest on crest, they pile up and reinforce; crest on trough, they cancel out. A bright band marks a spot where the waves from the two slits arrive perfectly in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ — and a dark band marks where they're half a wavelength out, (n + ½)λ, exactly like overlapping sound or water waves. The pattern is so regular that a single formula, Δx = λL/d on formula sheet, ties the fringe spacing to the wavelength. Which means Young had just measured the wavelength of light — with little more than a ruler.
veritasium.7: So what's actually waving? The answer came in the 1860s, built from physics that was already on the books. James Clerk Maxwell showed that a changing electric field makes a changing magnetic field, which remakes the electric field, and that this self-sustaining pair has no choice but to travel — at a speed his equations pinned at 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, the measured speed of light. That can't be a coincidence. Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave, thrown off wherever charges accelerate, and every electromagnetic wave — radio all the way to gamma — travels at the same speed c in a vacuum.
veritasium.8: And you've already met this in action. Electrons whipped around the Australian Synchrotron's storage ring are constantly accelerating — circular motion is acceleration — and accelerating charges radiate. That's why the machine pours out light. By 1900, with Young's pattern and Maxwell's fields telling the same story, the case looked closed.
veritasium.9: Connects The wave model gets the full treatment in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the interference evidence in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference. The fields behind it are the ones mapped in Unit 3.
veritasium.10: The experiment that cracked the case back open looks almost boring. Shine ultraviolet light on a clean metal surface, and electrons get kicked out of it — the photoelectric effect. That's it.
veritasium.11: Now, the wave model makes three confident predictions about this. Brighter light carries more energy, so the ejected electrons should come out faster. Any colour should work, as long as you give the metal time to soak up enough energy. And in dim light, there should be a delay while the energy piles up. All three sound completely reasonable.
veritasium.12: All three are wrong. The maximum kinetic energy of the electrons depends only on the light's frequency; crank up the brightness and you get more electrons, not faster ones. Below a threshold frequency, you get nothing at all — no matter how blindingly bright the light. And the emission is basically instant, even in the faintest glow.
veritasium.13: In 1905 Einstein offered an explanation the wave model just couldn't swallow: light hands over its energy in indivisible chunks. Each chunk — each photon — carries an energy given by E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck's constant, equal to 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. One photon dumps all its energy into one electron, which spends the metal's exit fee — the work function φ — and keeps whatever's left as kinetic energy: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet. You apply a reverse voltage and crank it up until even the fastest electron can't make it across — that's the stopping voltage — which is just W = qV not on sheet run backwards, a brake instead of an accelerator. And the electron-volt, the energy unit you first met when charges get accelerated through potential differences, becomes the everyday currency of everything that follows.
veritasium.15: Connects The photon and the full photoelectric analysis are worked through in chapter 14 · The photon; the stopping-voltage idea and the electron-volt were set up in Electric fields · Work and energy.
veritasium.16: In 1924 a French physicist named Louis de Broglie asked the obvious flip-side question — the one nobody had dared. If light, which everyone called a wave, can act like a stream of particles, then maybe matter, which everyone called particles, can act like a wave. He proposed that every moving object carries a wavelength, given by λ = h/p on formula sheet: the more momentum, the shorter the wave.

veritasium.34: Einstein's 1905 relativity papers ended with a short, almost throwaway result that turned out to matter more than all the rest: mass is just a concentrated form of energy. The rest energy locked inside any object is given by E0 = mc² on formula sheet, and because the conversion factor is c², a single teaspoon of water holds enough rest energy to power twenty thousand homes for a year. Set the object moving and its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, with the kinetic share given by Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet — and at everyday speeds this collapses right back into the familiar formula, one more approximation whose fine print finally makes sense.
veritasium.35: The Sun runs on exactly this conversion. Deep in its core, hydrogen fuses into helium, and the products weigh slightly less than what went in; that missing mass leaves as light. The Sun turns about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiated energy every second — sunlight is the Sun's own mass, converted at the universal rate.
veritasium.36: And it works both ways. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, both vanish and two gamma-ray photons fly off in exactly opposite directions — opposite, because momentum still has to balance. Hospital PET scanners image your body by catching exactly these photon pairs.
veritasium.37: In particle accelerators the conversion runs in reverse: the kinetic energy of a collision is turned into brand-new particles that simply didn't exist before the smash. The synchrotron you studied earlier as a tidy circular-motion exercise is, more honestly, a machine whose every magnet is engineered around γ.

What comes next is all the same material — just at greater depth.

attenborough.0: Here, in Unit 4 Area of Study 1, we come upon three great certainties — light as a wave, matter as particles, and space and time as fixed and unchanging — and the experiments that, one by one, toppled them. Read this as a single unfolding story. Nothing here asks to be solved.
attenborough.1: One question shapes everything that follows: what, truly, is light? Newton believed it a stream of minute particles, which he called corpuscles. His contemporary Huygens believed it a wave. And so the dispute ran on for more than a century, for both pictures explained the everyday evidence equally well — reflection, refraction, the casting of shadows.
attenborough.2: Consider what hangs in the balance. Almost everything we know of the universe — the temperature of a distant star, the makeup of a galaxy, the very structure of a virus — reaches us as light. Misunderstand light, and we misunderstand all that rides upon it.
attenborough.3: This Area of Study — its official question is How has understanding about the physical world changed? — unfolds in stages. Each begins with a settled, confident model, introduces one stubborn experimental result, and follows the model patiently to its undoing. Figure 1.1 gathers the evidence: stripes that no particle could ever produce, electrons flung out in a way no wave could explain, and particles of matter that trace out wave patterns. And by the end, even space and time themselves are made to give way.
attenborough.4: Connects The Fields material left us with a remarkable result: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field can give birth to one another, break free of every wire and charge, and travel on through empty space — see Where the field idea is heading. This page takes up that result, nurtures it — and then watches it overturned, not once but twice.
attenborough.5: In 1801 Thomas Young appeared to lay the long quarrel to rest. He sent light through two narrow slits onto a screen. Were light made of Newton’s corpuscles, two bright stripes ought to appear, one behind each slit. Instead the screen revealed many: a long, patient procession of evenly spaced bright and dark bands.
attenborough.6: Only a wave can do this. Where two ripples on a still pond meet crest upon crest, they swell together; crest upon trough, they cancel and vanish. A bright band marks the place where the waves from the two slits arrive in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ — and a dark band marks a half-wavelength of mismatch, (n + ½)λ, just as overlapping waves of sound and water behave. So regular is the pattern that a single formula, Δx = λL/d on formula sheet, ties the spacing of the fringes to the wavelength itself. And so Young had measured the wavelength of light with scarcely more than a ruler.
attenborough.7: What was it that waved? The answer came in the 1860s, drawn from ground already won. James Clerk Maxwell showed that a changing electric field gives rise to a changing magnetic field, which in turn recreates the electric field — and that this self-sustaining pair must travel, at a speed his equations fixed at 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, the very speed of light as measured. Light, then, can be pictured as a transverse electromagnetic wave, born wherever charges accelerate; and every electromagnetic wave — from radio to gamma — races at the same speed c through a vacuum.
attenborough.8: We have already met this in the wild. Electrons driven endlessly around the Australian Synchrotron’s storage ring are forever accelerating — for circular motion is acceleration — and accelerating charges radiate. That is why the machine pours out light. By 1900, with Young’s pattern and Maxwell’s fields in quiet agreement, the matter seemed settled for good.
attenborough.9: Connects The wave model is unfolded fully in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the interference evidence in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference. The fields at work are those we charted in Unit 3.
attenborough.10: The experiment that reopened it all seems, at first, almost too plain to matter. Shine ultraviolet light upon a clean metal surface, and electrons leap from it — the photoelectric effect.
attenborough.11: The wave model makes three confident predictions. Brighter light carries more energy, so the escaping electrons ought to leave faster. Any colour at all should work, given time enough for the metal to soak up the energy. And in dim light there should be a pause, a waiting, while the energy slowly gathers.
attenborough.12: Yet experiment defied every one. The greatest kinetic energy of the electrons depends only on the light’s frequency; turning up the brightness sends out more electrons, never faster ones. Below a certain threshold frequency, not a single electron stirs, no matter how dazzling the light. And the emission is all but instant, even in the faintest glow.
attenborough.13: In 1905 Einstein offered an account the wave model simply could not hold: light delivers its energy in indivisible parcels. Each parcel — each photon — carries an energy given by E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck’s constant, equal to 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. One photon hands all its energy to one electron, which spends the metal’s toll of escape — the work function φ — and keeps whatever remains as kinetic energy: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet. Every observation flows from this: frequency sets the energy of each parcel, brightness sets only how many parcels arrive, and a parcel is handed over whole, or not at all.
attenborough.14: The way we measure it comes straight from Unit 3. To find the electrons’ energy, a reverse voltage is applied and raised until even the swiftest electron can no longer cross — the stopping voltage — which is W = qV not on sheet turned around, a brake rather than an accelerator. The electron-volt — that unit of energy born when charges are flung through potential differences — becomes the working currency of all that follows.
attenborough.15: Connects The photon and the full photoelectric story are taken up in chapter 14 · The photon; the stopping-voltage idea and the electron-volt were established back in Electric fields · Work and energy.
attenborough.16: In 1924 a French physicist, Louis de Broglie, dared to ask the question the other way round. If light — long held to be a wave — can behave as a stream of particles, then might matter — long held to be particles — behave as a wave? He proposed that every moving thing carries a wavelength of its own, given by λ = h/p on formula sheet: the greater the momentum, the shorter the wave.
attenborough.17: Three years on, the test was made: a beam of electrons fired at a thin crystal. From it emerged concentric rings upon a screen — the very same family of pattern that X-rays of equal wavelength paint through the same crystal, as panel (c) of Figure 1.1 reveals. The electron — that humble particle we accelerated with voltages and curved into circles with magnetic fields in Unit 3 — diffracts.
attenborough.18: A cricket ball does not diffract through a doorway, because h is so vanishingly small. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ has a de Broglie wavelength of about 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m — far tinier than any gap it might slip through, and diffraction shows itself only when the wavelength rivals the opening. Electrons are light enough that their wavelengths match the spacing between atoms, which is why crystals serve them as marvellously fine diffraction gratings.
attenborough.19: The most profound version of the experiment is the one Simulation 1.1 lays bare. Electrons — or photons — are sent through Young’s double slits one at a time. Each arrives at the screen as a single tiny dot: a particle. Yet as thousands of dots gather, the interference fringes rise out of the statistics: a wave. No single particle makes a pattern — and yet every one of them obeys it.
attenborough.20: Connects Matter waves are taken up in chapter 15 · Matter waves. The electron beam at their heart is the very one built in Motors & accelerators — the same electrons, cast in a new role.
attenborough.21: Two puzzles about atoms had long gone unanswered, waiting in the wings. First, by the classical reckoning atoms ought not to be stable at all: a classical electron circling a nucleus is accelerating, and accelerating charges radiate — Maxwell’s own rule, the very one that lights the synchrotron — so the electron should bleed away its energy and spiral into the nucleus in a fraction of a second.
attenborough.22: Second, glowing gases do not pour out a continuous spectrum. Each element gives only certain sharp colours — a line spectrum, a signature all its own. Sodium street lamps shine their two yellow lines; metal vapour lamps and neon signs each flaunt their own; and sunlight carries thousands of dark lines, where cooler gas has stolen away exactly those colours.
attenborough.23: The answer is quantisation. An electron bound within an atom may hold only certain energies — discrete levels, with nothing whatsoever in between. A fall from a higher level to a lower one makes the atom emit a single photon whose energy E = hf matches the gap precisely; and a photon of exactly a gap’s energy, swallowed, lifts the electron to a higher level. Bright emission lines and dark absorption lines are the same ladder of levels, climbed in opposite directions — and so a spectrum lays bare the atom’s energy levels for all to read.
attenborough.24: De Broglie’s wave, too, explains why these levels exist at all. When the electron’s wave is wrapped about the atom, it must close upon itself smoothly — a standing wave, like a string that sounds only its harmonics. Only certain wavelengths fit the loop, so only certain energies are allowed.
attenborough.25: It is a simplified picture, later refined by deeper physics — but it accounts for the one thing the classical atom never could: why an atom has a lowest rung, a ground state, below which the electron cannot fall, and so why atoms endure at all. Quantisation is not the province of light alone; it is woven into the very fabric of matter.
attenborough.26: Connects Spectra and energy levels are taken up in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; the standing waves that carve out those levels are first met alongside light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
attenborough.27: One question still lingered from the wave model’s first triumph. Every wave known to physics waves in something — so what, then, carries light across the emptiness of space? Physicists named the imagined medium the aether, and in 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument fine enough to catch it: two light beams ran along perpendicular arms, recombined to interfere, and the whole apparatus was turned and tested again and again through the seasons, as the Earth swept around the Sun. If the Earth moves through an aether, the beam times should differ. They never did — the most celebrated null result in all of physics.
attenborough.28: In 1905 — the same year as the photon — Einstein took the result exactly as it stood and rebuilt mechanics from two postulates: that the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and that the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, whatever the motion of the source or the observer. The second sounds unremarkable. It is not. For if every observer measures the same c, then time and distance themselves must bend and differ from one observer to the next.
attenborough.29: Their consequences carry names we shall use all year. Moving clocks run slow — time dilation, given by t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths shrink along the direction of travel — length contraction, given by L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
attenborough.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, sits only a whisker above 1 at everyday speeds — which is why the effect lay hidden for three centuries — and climbs without limit as the speed creeps toward c. That little qualification “v << c” pinned to Unit 3’s circular-path formula was quietly pointing at this very condition, a whole unit ahead of time.
attenborough.31: The evidence rains down from the cosmos itself. Muons — heavy, fleeting cousins of the electron, conjured kilometres overhead by cosmic rays — live on average a mere 2.2 μs. Even at 0.995c that would carry them but 660 m, and yet muons reach the ground in their multitudes. In the ground frame of reference their inner clocks run about ten times slow, so they endure long enough to finish the journey; in the muon’s own frame of reference the atmosphere is contracted about tenfold, so the journey is short enough to finish — two tellings, one and the same outcome.
attenborough.32: The satellites that pinpoint your phone carry clocks that run slow by a few microseconds each day on account of their orbital speed; left uncorrected, GPS positions would wander off by kilometres a day. Your phone applies special relativity every single time it finds where you are.
attenborough.33: Connects The postulates, proper time and the muon evidence are unfolded in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. The “v << c” qualification was first met on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
attenborough.34: Einstein’s relativity papers of 1905 closed with a brief result that would outshine all the rest: mass is energy, locked away in concentrated form. The rest energy held within any object is given by E0 = mc² on formula sheet, and because the conversion factor is c², a single teaspoon of water holds rest energy enough to light twenty thousand homes for a year. Set the object moving, and its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, with the kinetic share given by Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet — and at everyday speeds this folds back into the familiar formula, one last approximation whose fine print is now plain to see.
attenborough.35: The Sun lives by this very exchange. Deep in its core, hydrogen fuses into helium, and the products weigh a fraction less than the reactants; the missing mass streams away as light. Each second the Sun turns about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiated energy — sunlight is the Sun’s own mass, converted at the universal rate.
attenborough.36: And the exchange runs both ways. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, both are annihilated, and two gamma-ray photons fly apart in opposite directions — opposite, because momentum must still keep its balance. Hospital PET scanners peer inside the body by catching exactly these pairs of photons.
attenborough.37: In particle accelerators the exchange is driven into reverse: the kinetic energy of a collision is forged into new particles that simply did not exist before the impact. The synchrotron we met earlier as a tidy circular-motion exercise is, more truly, a machine whose every magnet is built around γ.
attenborough.38: Connects The full energy account is unfolded in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that pour out that energy were taken up in Motors & accelerators.
attenborough.39: And so the whole Area of Study has now been travelled once: a wave that proved to arrive in discrete parcels, particles that proved to interfere, atoms held together by standing waves, and a speed limit that bends time and length and unmasks mass as concentrated energy. What lies ahead is the same country, explored in far greater depth.

greekepic.0: Unit 4 Area of Study 1 tells of three sovereign models held mighty for an age — light enthroned as a wave, matter ruled as particles, and space and time decreed unmoving — and of the trials by which each was cast down. Read it as a single epic told in one breath. Nothing here asks to be solved; only to be heard.
greekepic.1: The question that governs this whole song is the nature of light. Newton declared it a stream of tiny bodies, which he named corpuscles. His rival Huygens proclaimed it a wave. The quarrel raged for more than a hundred years, for both champions could account for the daily testimony of reflection, refraction and shadow.
greekepic.2: The stakes are vast: nearly all that mortals know of the universe — the fire of distant stars, the substance of galaxies, the architecture of a virus — arrives borne on light. A false account of light breeds a false account of all that rests upon it.
greekepic.3: This Area of Study — whose appointed question is How has understanding about the physical world changed? — advances in stages, as a tale told in cantos.
